title: Colorado River

infobox.value: [Glenwood Springs, CO](/wiki/Glenwood_Springs,_Colorado "Glenwood Springs, Colorado"), [Grand Junction, CO](/wiki/Grand_Junction,_Colorado "Grand Junction, Colorado"), [Moab, UT](/wiki/Moab,_Utah "Moab, Utah"), [Page, AZ](/wiki/Page,_Arizona "Page, Arizona"), [Bullhead City, AZ](/wiki/Bullhead_City,_Arizona "Bullhead City, Arizona"), [Lake Havasu City, AZ](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"), [Blythe, CA](/wiki/Blythe,_California "Blythe, California"), [Yuma, AZ](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"), [Las Vegas, NV](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"), [Laughlin, NV](/wiki/Laughlin,_Nevada "Laughlin, Nevada"), [San Luis Río Colorado, SON](/wiki/San_Luis_Rio_Colorado "San Luis Rio Colorado")

[Fraser River](/wiki/Fraser_River_(Colorado) "Fraser River (Colorado)"), [Blue River](/wiki/Blue_River_(Colorado) "Blue River (Colorado)"), [Eagle River](/wiki/Eagle_River_(Colorado) "Eagle River (Colorado)"), [Roaring Fork River](/wiki/Roaring_Fork_River "Roaring Fork River"), [Gunnison River](/wiki/Gunnison_River "Gunnison River"), [Dolores River](/wiki/Dolores_River "Dolores River"), [San Juan River](/wiki/San_Juan_River_(Colorado_River) "San Juan River (Colorado River)"), [Little Colorado River](/wiki/Little_Colorado_River "Little Colorado River"), [Bill Williams River](/wiki/Bill_Williams_River "Bill Williams River"), [Gila River](/wiki/Gila_River "Gila River")

[Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)"), [Dirty Devil River](/wiki/Dirty_Devil_River "Dirty Devil River"), [Escalante River](/wiki/Escalante_River "Escalante River"), [Kanab River](/wiki/Kanab_River "Kanab River"), [Virgin River](/wiki/Virgin_River "Virgin River"), [Hardy River](/wiki/Hardy_River "Hardy River")

([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Río Colorado*) is one of the principal [rivers](/wiki/River "River") (along with the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande")) in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") and in [northern Mexico](/wiki/Northern_Mexico "Northern Mexico"). The 1,450-mile-long (2,330 km) river, the [5th longest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_longest_rivers_of_the_United_States_(by_main_stem) "List of longest rivers of the United States (by main stem)"), drains an expansive, arid [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") that encompasses parts of seven U.S. states and two Mexican states. The name Colorado derives from the Spanish language for "colored reddish" due to its heavy [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt") load. Starting in the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), it flows generally southwest across the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau") and through the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") before reaching [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead") on the [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona")–[Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") border, where it turns south toward the [international border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). After entering Mexico, the Colorado approaches the mostly dry [Colorado River Delta](/wiki/Colorado_River_Delta "Colorado River Delta") at the tip of the [Gulf of California](/wiki/Gulf_of_California "Gulf of California") between [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") and [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Major river in the western United States and Mexico

This article is about the river in the Southwestern United States and Mexico. For the river in Texas, see [Colorado River (Texas)](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"). The **Colorado River** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Río Colorado*) is one of the principal [rivers](/wiki/River "River") (along with the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande")) in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") and in [northern Mexico](/wiki/Northern_Mexico "Northern Mexico"). The 1,450-mile-long (2,330 km) river, the [5th longest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_longest_rivers_of_the_United_States_(by_main_stem) "List of longest rivers of the United States (by main stem)"), drains an expansive, arid [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") that encompasses parts of seven U.S. states and two Mexican states. The name Colorado derives from the Spanish language for "colored reddish" due to its heavy [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt") load. Starting in the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), it flows generally southwest across the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau") and through the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") before reaching [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead") on the [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona")–[Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") border, where it turns south toward the [international border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). After entering Mexico, the Colorado approaches the mostly dry [Colorado River Delta](/wiki/Colorado_River_Delta "Colorado River Delta") at the tip of the [Gulf of California](/wiki/Gulf_of_California "Gulf of California") between [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") and [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora").

Known for its dramatic canyons, whitewater rapids, and eleven [U.S. National Parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_the_United_States "National parks of the United States"), the Colorado River and its tributaries are a vital source of water for 40 million people.[[4]](#cite_note-leesferrycompact-4) An [extensive system of dams](/wiki/List_of_dams_in_the_Colorado_River_system "List of dams in the Colorado River system"), reservoirs, and aqueducts divert almost its entire flow for agricultural [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") and urban water supply.[[5]](#cite_note-Waterman2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ImperialEB-6)[[7]](#cite_note-san_juan-7) Its large flow and steep gradient are used to generate [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"), meeting [peaking power](/wiki/Peaking_power_plant "Peaking power plant") demands in much of the [Intermountain West](/wiki/Intermountain_West "Intermountain West"). Intensive water consumption has dried up the lower 100 miles (160 km) of the river, which has rarely reached the sea since the 1960s.[[5]](#cite_note-Waterman2-5)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Sacred-9)

[Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") have inhabited the Colorado River basin for at least 8,000 years. Starting around 1 CE, large agriculture-based societies were established, but a combination of drought and poor land use practices led to their collapse in the 1300s. Their descendants include tribes such as the [Puebloans](/wiki/Puebloans "Puebloans"), while others including the [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo") settled in the Colorado Basin after the 1000s. In the 1500s, [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") explorers began mapping and claiming the watershed, which became part of Mexico upon winning its [independence from Spain](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence") in 1821. Even after most of the watershed became US territory in 1846, much of the river's course remained unknown. Several expeditions charted the Colorado in the mid-19th century—one of which, led by [John Wesley Powell](/wiki/John_Wesley_Powell "John Wesley Powell"), was the first to run the rapids of the Grand Canyon. Large-scale settlement of the lower basin began in the mid- to late-1800s, with [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") sailing from the Gulf of California to landings along the river that linked to wagon roads to the interior. Starting in the 1860s, gold and silver strikes drew prospectors to the upper Colorado River basin.

Large-scale river management began in the early 1900s, with major guidelines established in a series of international and US interstate treaties known as the "[Law of the River](/wiki/Colorado_River_Compact "Colorado River Compact")". The US federal government constructed most of the major dams and aqueducts between 1910 and 1970; the largest, [Hoover Dam](/wiki/Hoover_Dam "Hoover Dam"), was completed in 1935. Numerous water projects have also involved state and local governments. With all of their waters fully allocated, both the Colorado and the neighboring Rio Grande are now considered among the most controlled and litigated river systems in the world. Since 2000, extended drought has conflicted with increasing demands for Colorado River water, and the level of human development and control of the river continues to generate controversy.

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Course")]

Main article: [Course of the Colorado River](/wiki/Course_of_the_Colorado_River "Course of the Colorado River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Colorado_River_in_Rocky_Mountain_NP.jpg/250px-Colorado_River_in_Rocky_Mountain_NP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colorado_River_in_Rocky_Mountain_NP.jpg)

Headwaters of the Colorado River in [Rocky Mountain National Park](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_National_Park "Rocky Mountain National Park"), Colorado

The Colorado begins as many tributaries in Wyoming and Colorado, the largest being the Green River. The Colorado River itself starts in Rocky Mountain National Park then makes a short run south, then the river turns west below [Grand Lake](/wiki/Grand_Lake_(Colorado) "Grand Lake (Colorado)"), the largest natural lake in Colorado state.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg200938-10) For the first 250 miles (400 km) of its course, the Colorado carves its way through the mountainous [Western Slope](/wiki/Colorado_Western_Slope "Colorado Western Slope"), a sparsely populated region defined by the portion of the state west of the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas"). As it flows southwest, it gains strength from many small tributaries, as well as larger ones including the [Blue](/wiki/Blue_River_(Colorado) "Blue River (Colorado)"), [Eagle](/wiki/Eagle_River_(Colorado) "Eagle River (Colorado)") and [Roaring Fork](/wiki/Roaring_Fork_River "Roaring Fork River") rivers. After passing through [De Beque Canyon](/wiki/De_Beque_Canyon "De Beque Canyon"), the Colorado emerges from the Rockies into the [Grand Valley](/wiki/Grand_Valley_(Colorado-Utah) "Grand Valley (Colorado-Utah)"), a major farming and ranching region where it meets one of its largest tributaries, the [Gunnison River](/wiki/Gunnison_River "Gunnison River"), at [Grand Junction](/wiki/Grand_Junction,_Colorado "Grand Junction, Colorado"). Most of the upper river is a swift whitewater stream ranging from 200 to 500 feet (60 to 150 m) wide, the depth ranging from 6 to 30 feet (2 to 9 m), with a few notable exceptions, such as the Blackrocks [reach](/wiki/Reach_(geography) "Reach (geography)") where the river is nearly 100 feet (30 m) deep.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005490-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Pitlick-12) In a few areas, such as the marshy Kawuneeche Valley near the headwaters[[13]](#cite_note-13) and the Grand Valley, it exhibits [braided](/wiki/Braided_stream "Braided stream") characteristics.[[12]](#cite_note-Pitlick-12)

From Grand Junction, the Colorado turns northwest before cutting southwest across the eponymous [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau"), a vast area of high desert centered at the [Four Corners](/wiki/Four_Corners "Four Corners") of the southwestern United States. Here, the climate becomes significantly drier than that in the Rocky Mountains, and the river becomes entrenched in progressively deeper gorges of bare rock, beginning with [Ruby Canyon](/wiki/Ruby_Canyon "Ruby Canyon") and then [Westwater Canyon](/wiki/Westwater_Canyon "Westwater Canyon") as it enters [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), now once again heading southwest.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Farther downstream it receives the [Dolores River](/wiki/Dolores_River "Dolores River") and defines the southern border of [Arches National Park](/wiki/Arches_National_Park "Arches National Park"), before passing [Moab](/wiki/Moab,_Utah "Moab, Utah") and flowing through "The Portal", where it exits the Moab Valley between a pair of 1,000-foot (300 m) sandstone cliffs.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![A narrow river flows through a narrow gorge flanked by high rocky bluffs](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Grand_Canyon_view_from_Pima_Point_2010.jpg/250px-Grand_Canyon_view_from_Pima_Point_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Canyon_view_from_Pima_Point_2010.jpg)

Colorado River in the Grand Canyon seen from Pima Point, near [Hermit's Rest](/wiki/Hermit%27s_Rest "Hermit's Rest")

In Utah, the Colorado flows primarily through the "[slickrock](/wiki/Entrada_Sandstone "Entrada Sandstone")" country, which is characterized by its narrow canyons and unique "folds" created by the tilting of sedimentary rock layers along faults. This is one of the most inaccessible regions of the continental United States.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHopkins2002118-17) Below the confluence with the [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)"), its largest tributary, in [Canyonlands National Park](/wiki/Canyonlands_National_Park "Canyonlands National Park"), the Colorado enters [Cataract Canyon](/wiki/Cataract_Canyon "Cataract Canyon"), named for its dangerous rapids,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Cott199070-18) and then [Glen Canyon](/wiki/Glen_Canyon "Glen Canyon"), known for its arches and erosion-sculpted [Navajo sandstone](/wiki/Navajo_sandstone "Navajo sandstone") formations.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Cott1990155-19) Here, the [San Juan River](/wiki/San_Juan_River_(Colorado_River) "San Juan River (Colorado River)"), carrying runoff from the southern slope of Colorado's [San Juan Mountains](/wiki/San_Juan_Mountains "San Juan Mountains"), joins the Colorado from the east. The Colorado then enters northern [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), where since the 1960s [Glen Canyon Dam](/wiki/Glen_Canyon_Dam "Glen Canyon Dam") near [Page](/wiki/Page,_Arizona "Page, Arizona") has flooded the Glen Canyon reach of the river, forming [Lake Powell](/wiki/Lake_Powell "Lake Powell") for [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") generation.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In Arizona, the river passes [Lee's Ferry](/wiki/Lee%27s_Ferry "Lee's Ferry"), an important crossing for early explorers and settlers and since the early 20th century the principal point where Colorado River flows are measured for apportionment to the seven U.S. and two Mexican states in the basin.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Downstream, the river enters [Marble Canyon](/wiki/Marble_Canyon "Marble Canyon"), the beginning of the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon"), passing under the [Navajo Bridges](/wiki/Navajo_Bridge "Navajo Bridge") on a now southward course. Below the confluence with the [Little Colorado River](/wiki/Little_Colorado_River "Little Colorado River"), the river swings west into Granite Gorge, the most dramatic portion of the Grand Canyon, where the river cuts up to one mile (1.6 km) into the Colorado Plateau, exposing some of the oldest visible rocks on Earth, dating as long ago as 2 billion years.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The 277 miles (446 km) of the river that flow through the Grand Canyon are largely encompassed by [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park") and are known for their difficult whitewater, separated by pools that reach up to 110 feet (34 m) in depth.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

At the lower end of Grand Canyon, the Colorado widens into [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead"), the largest reservoir in the continental United States, formed by [Hoover Dam](/wiki/Hoover_Dam "Hoover Dam") on the border of Arizona and [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"). Situated southeast of metropolitan [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"), the dam is an integral component for management of the Colorado River, controlling floods and storing water for farms and cities in the lower Colorado River basin.[[25]](#cite_note-LakeMead-25) Below the dam the river passes under the [Mike O'Callaghan–Pat Tillman Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Mike_O%27Callaghan%E2%80%93Pat_Tillman_Memorial_Bridge "Mike O'Callaghan–Pat Tillman Memorial Bridge")—which at nearly 900 feet (270 m) above the water is the highest concrete [arch bridge](/wiki/Arch_bridge "Arch bridge") in the Western Hemisphere[[26]](#cite_note-26)—and then turns due south towards Mexico, defining the Arizona–Nevada and Arizona–[California](/wiki/California "California") borders.

[![Top-down view of green agricultural lands surrounded by desert](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Yuma_arizona_map.jpg/250px-Yuma_arizona_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yuma_arizona_map.jpg)

Satellite view of the Colorado River valley near [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"); [Interstate 8](/wiki/Interstate_8 "Interstate 8") runs from left to right just below center.

After leaving the confines of the [Black Canyon](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Colorado "Black Canyon of the Colorado"), the river emerges from the Colorado Plateau into the [Lower Colorado River Valley](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Valley "Lower Colorado River Valley") (LCRV), a desert region dependent on irrigation agriculture and tourism and also home to several major [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The river widens here to a broad, moderately deep waterway averaging 500 to 1,000 feet (150 to 300 m) wide and reaching up to 0.25 miles (400 m) across, with depths ranging from 8 to 60 feet (2 to 20 m).[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Before [channelization](/wiki/River_engineering#Channelization "River engineering") of the Colorado in the 20th century, the lower river was subject to frequent course changes caused by seasonal flow variations. [Joseph C. Ives](/wiki/Joseph_C._Ives "Joseph C. Ives"), who surveyed the lower river in 1861, wrote that "the shifting of the channel, the banks, the islands, the bars is so continual and rapid that a detailed description, derived from the experiences of one trip, would be found incorrect, not only during the subsequent year, but perhaps in the course of a week, or even a day."[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta2007204-30)

The LCRV is one of the most densely populated areas along the river, and there are numerous towns including [Bullhead City, Arizona](/wiki/Bullhead_City,_Arizona "Bullhead City, Arizona"), [Needles, California](/wiki/Needles,_California "Needles, California"), and [Lake Havasu City, Arizona](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"). Here, several large diversions draw from the river, providing water for both local uses and distant regions including the [Salt River Valley](/wiki/Salt_River_Valley "Salt River Valley") of Arizona and metropolitan [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California").[[31]](#cite_note-31) The last major U.S. diversion is at [Imperial Dam](/wiki/Imperial_Dam "Imperial Dam"), where over 90 percent of the river's flow is moved into the Gila Gravity Canal and Yuma Area Project, and the much bigger [All-American Canal](/wiki/All-American_Canal "All-American Canal") to irrigate California's [Imperial Valley](/wiki/Imperial_Valley "Imperial Valley"), the most productive winter agricultural region in the United States.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/ColoradoRiverAtSanLuis.jpg/250px-ColoradoRiverAtSanLuis.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColoradoRiverAtSanLuis.jpg)

Colorado River as it exits the United States into Mexico beneath the San Luis Colorado-Colonia Miguel Alemán Bridge (September 2009)

Below Imperial Dam, only a small portion of the Colorado River makes it beyond [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"), and the confluence with the intermittent [Gila River](/wiki/Gila_River "Gila River")—which carries runoff from western [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") and most of Arizona–before defining 24 miles (39 km) of the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). At [Morelos Dam](/wiki/Morelos_Dam "Morelos Dam"), the entire remaining flow of the Colorado is diverted to irrigate the [Mexicali Valley](/wiki/Mexicali_Valley "Mexicali Valley"), among Mexico's most fertile agricultural lands.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Below [San Luis Río Colorado](/wiki/San_Luis_R%C3%ADo_Colorado "San Luis Río Colorado"), the Colorado passes entirely into Mexico, defining the [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California")–[Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora") border. Since 1960, the stretch of the Colorado between here and the Gulf of California has been dry or a trickle formed by irrigation return flows. The [Hardy River](/wiki/Hardy_River "Hardy River") provides most of the flow into the [Colorado River Delta](/wiki/Colorado_River_Delta "Colorado River Delta"), a vast alluvial floodplain covering about 3,000 square miles (7,800 km2) of northwestern Mexico.[[34]](#cite_note-34) A large [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") is formed here before the Colorado empties into the Gulf about 75 miles (120 km) south of Yuma. Occasionally the [International Boundary and Water Commission](/wiki/International_Boundary_and_Water_Commission "International Boundary and Water Commission") allows a springtime [pulse flow](/w/index.php?title=Pulse_flow&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pulse flow (page does not exist)") to recharge the delta.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Before 20th-century development dewatered the lower Colorado, a major [tidal bore](/wiki/Tidal_bore "Tidal bore") was present in the delta and estuary; the first historical record was made by the [Croatian](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") missionary in Spanish service Father [Ferdinand Konščak](/wiki/Ferdinand_Kon%C5%A1%C4%8Dak "Ferdinand Konščak") on July 18, 1746.[[36]](#cite_note-Chanson-36) During [spring tide](/wiki/Spring_tide "Spring tide") conditions, the tidal bore—locally called *El Burro*—formed in the estuary about [Montague Island](/wiki/Montague_Island_(Baja_California) "Montague Island (Baja California)") in Baja California and propagated upstream.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFelgerBroyles2007519–520-37)

### Major tributaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Major tributaries")]

[![View of a river winding through a series of narrow gorges that dramatically double back on each other](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/San_juan_river_entrenched_meanders.jpg/250px-San_juan_river_entrenched_meanders.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_juan_river_entrenched_meanders.jpg)

The San Juan River near [Mexican Hat, Utah](/wiki/Mexican_Hat,_Utah "Mexican Hat, Utah")

[![View of a brownish river flowing between vegetated banks, with high bluffs rising in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Mineral-Bottom.jpg/250px-Mineral-Bottom.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mineral-Bottom.jpg)

The Green River at Mineral Bottom, just north of Canyonlands National Park

Main article: [List of tributaries of the Colorado River](/wiki/List_of_tributaries_of_the_Colorado_River "List of tributaries of the Colorado River")

The Colorado is joined by over 25 significant tributaries, of which the Green River is the largest by both length and discharge. The Green River takes drainage from the [Wind River Range](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range") of west-central Wyoming, from Utah's [Uinta Mountains](/wiki/Uinta_Mountains "Uinta Mountains"), and from the Rockies of northwestern Colorado.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The Gila River is the second longest and drains a greater area than the Green,[[39]](#cite_note-USGSbasins-39) but has a significantly lower flow because of a more arid climate and larger diversions for irrigation and cities.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005514-40) Both the Gunnison and San Juan rivers, which derive most of their water from Rocky Mountains snowmelt, contribute more water than the Gila contributed naturally.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005532–533-41)

## Drainage basin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Drainage basin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/CRBS_Executive_Summary_FINAL-01.png/250px-CRBS_Executive_Summary_FINAL-01.png)](/wiki/File:CRBS_Executive_Summary_FINAL-01.png)

Map showing the Upper and Lower Colorado River Basin, and adjacent areas supplied by Colorado River water.

The **Colorado River Basin** consists of 246,000 square miles (640,000 km2), making it the seventh largest drainage basin in North America.[[1]](#cite_note-USGSrivers-1) About 238,600 square miles (618,000 km2), or 97 percent of the basin, is in the United States.[[39]](#cite_note-USGSbasins-39) The basin extends into western Colorado and New Mexico, southwestern [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), eastern and southern Utah, southeastern Nevada and California, and most of Arizona. The areas drained within Baja California and Sonora in Mexico are very small and do not contribute significant runoff.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005483–486-58) Aside from the Colorado River Delta, the basin extends into Sonora at a few locations further east, including the headwaters of the [Santa Cruz River](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_River_(Arizona) "Santa Cruz River (Arizona)") and [San Pedro River](/wiki/San_Pedro_River_(Arizona) "San Pedro River (Arizona)") (both tributaries of the Gila River).[[55]](#cite_note-watershedmap-59) For hydrological management purposes, the Colorado River Basin is divided into the **Upper Basin** (the drainage area above Lees Ferry), and the **Lower Basin**. The Upper Basin covers only 45 percent of the land area of the Colorado River Basin, but contributes 92 percent of the runoff.[[56]](#cite_note-Lukas-60)

The entire eastern boundary of the Colorado River Basin runs along the [North American Continental Divide](/wiki/North_American_Continental_Divide "North American Continental Divide") and is defined largely by the Rocky Mountains and the [Rio Grande Basin](/w/index.php?title=Rio_Grande_Basin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rio Grande Basin (page does not exist)"). The [Wind River Range](/wiki/Wind_River_Range "Wind River Range") in Wyoming marks the northern extent of the basin, and is separated from the Colorado Rockies by the endorheic [Great Divide Basin](/wiki/Great_Divide_Basin "Great Divide Basin") in southwestern Wyoming. Streams that are nearby the east side of the divide drain into the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande"), while nearby areas north of the Wind River Range drain into the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River").[[55]](#cite_note-watershedmap-59) The western boundary of the Colorado River Basin is formed by various ranges and plateaus that border the [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin"), including the [Uinta Mountains](/wiki/Uinta_Mountains "Uinta Mountains") and [Wasatch Range](/wiki/Wasatch_Range "Wasatch Range"). Major Great Basin watersheds bordering the Colorado River Basin are the [Great Salt Lake](/wiki/Great_Salt_Lake "Great Salt Lake") and [Sevier Lake](/wiki/Sevier_Lake "Sevier Lake") watersheds.[[55]](#cite_note-watershedmap-59) To the south, the Colorado River Basin borders several watersheds in Mexico draining into the Gulf of California, including the [Sonoyta](/wiki/Sonoyta_River "Sonoyta River"), [Concepción](/wiki/Concepci%C3%B3n_River "Concepción River"), and [Yaqui](/wiki/Yaqui_River "Yaqui River") rivers.[[55]](#cite_note-watershedmap-59)[[57]](#cite_note-61) Much of the basin is at high elevation; the mean elevation is 5,500 feet (1,700 m).[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005483–486-58)[[58]](#cite_note-62) Lees Ferry, more than halfway along the Colorado River from its source, is 3,150 feet (960 m) above sea level.[[59]](#cite_note-63) The highest point in the Colorado River Basin is 14,321-foot (4,365 m) [Uncompahgre Peak](/wiki/Uncompahgre_Peak "Uncompahgre Peak") in Colorado's [San Juan Mountains](/wiki/San_Juan_Mountains "San Juan Mountains"), while some water from the river drains via irrigation run-off into California's [Salton Sea](/wiki/Salton_Sea "Salton Sea"), 236 feet (72 m) below sea level.[[60]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Grand_Canyon_National_Park_Colorado_River_Black_Bridge_0618_%286697170671%29.jpg/250px-Grand_Canyon_National_Park_Colorado_River_Black_Bridge_0618_%286697170671%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Canyon_National_Park_Colorado_River_Black_Bridge_0618_(6697170671).jpg)

The [Black Suspension Bridge](/wiki/Black_Suspension_Bridge "Black Suspension Bridge") crosses the Colorado River in the Grand Canyon at [Phantom Ranch](/wiki/Phantom_Ranch "Phantom Ranch"), Arizona.

About 72 percent of the Colorado River Basin is classified as arid, with the [Sonoran](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert") and [Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") deserts covering the southern portion and the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau") encompassing much of the central portion.[[61]](#cite_note-landuse-65) The Colorado Plateau is home to most of the major canyon systems formed by the Colorado River and its tributaries, particularly those of the Green and San Juan rivers. About 23 percent of the basin is forest, with the largest area in the Rocky Mountains; other significant forested areas include the [Kaibab](/wiki/Kaibab_Plateau "Kaibab Plateau"), [Aquarius](/wiki/Aquarius_Plateau "Aquarius Plateau"), and [Markagunt](/wiki/Markagunt_Plateau "Markagunt Plateau") plateaus in southern Utah and northern Arizona, and the [Mogollon Rim](/wiki/Mogollon_Rim "Mogollon Rim") in central Arizona.[[61]](#cite_note-landuse-65) These high plateaus and escarpments, often exceeding 9,000 feet (2,700 m) in elevation, form the northern and southern edges of the Colorado Plateau geological province.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005483–489-66)[[63]](#cite_note-67) Developed land use in the basin is mostly irrigated agriculture, chiefly in the [Grand Valley](/wiki/Grand_Valley_(Colorado-Utah) "Grand Valley (Colorado-Utah)"), the [Lower Colorado River Valley](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Valley "Lower Colorado River Valley"), and the [Salt River Valley](/wiki/Salt_River_Valley "Salt River Valley"), but the total area of crop and pasture land is only 2–3 percent of the entire basin.[[61]](#cite_note-landuse-65) Urban areas cover less than 1 percent.[[61]](#cite_note-landuse-65)

Climate in the Colorado River Basin ranges from subtropical [hot desert](/wiki/Hot_desert_climate "Hot desert climate") at southern, lower elevations to [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") in the Rocky Mountains.[[64]](#cite_note-NAP_climate-68) Mean monthly high temperatures are 25.3 °C (77.5 °F) in the Upper Basin and 33.4 °C (92.1 °F) in the Lower Basin, and lows average −3.6 and 8.9 °C (25.5 and 48.0 °F), respectively. Annual precipitation averages 6.5 inches (164 mm), ranging from over 60 inches (1,500 mm) in some areas of the Rockies to less than 4 inches (100 mm) in dry desert valleys.[[56]](#cite_note-Lukas-60) The Upper Basin generally receives snow and rain during the winter and early spring, while precipitation in the Lower Basin falls mainly during intense but infrequent summer thunderstorms brought on by the [North American Monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon").[[65]](#cite_note-69) Precipitation is influenced by the [El Niño-Southern Oscillation](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o-Southern_Oscillation "El Niño-Southern Oscillation") (ENSO) with [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") being associated with wetter conditions and [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña") with drier conditions. The effect of ENSO is significantly more pronounced in the Lower Basin, where it has a strong impact on monsoonal rainfall.[[56]](#cite_note-Lukas-60) Runoff patterns across the Colorado River Basin reflect this; most of the perennial tributaries originate in the Upper Basin, while tributaries in the Lower Basin are either [ephemeral](/wiki/Ephemeral_stream "Ephemeral stream") (such as the Little Colorado River) or highly seasonal (such as the Gila and Salt rivers).[[56]](#cite_note-Lukas-60)

As of 2010, approximately 13 million people lived within the Colorado River basin,[[n 5]](#cite_note-73) while about 40 million people live in areas supplied by Colorado River water. Colorado River basin states are among the fastest-growing in the US; the population of Nevada alone increased by about 66 percent between 1990 and 2000 as Arizona grew by some 40 percent.[[69]](#cite_note-74) [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada") and [Mexicali, Baja California](/wiki/Mexicali,_Baja_California "Mexicali, Baja California") are the largest cities by population within the Colorado River Basin. Other significant cities include [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), [St. George, Utah](/wiki/St._George,_Utah "St. George, Utah") and [Flagstaff, Arizona](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona"). Due to the rugged and inhospitable topography through which the river flows, there are only a few major towns along the Colorado River itself, including [Grand Junction, Colorado](/wiki/Grand_Junction,_Colorado "Grand Junction, Colorado") and [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005483–486-58)

## Discharge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Discharge")]

[![The average annual discharge of the Colorado River has shown a slight but noticeable decreasing trend between 1895 and 2004.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b1/Coloradoflowgraph.png)](/wiki/File:Coloradoflowgraph.png)

Annual Colorado River discharge volumes at Lee's Ferry between 1895 and 2004

The unimpaired annual runoff[[n 6]](#cite_note-76) of the Colorado River at Lees Ferry, Arizona is estimated at 14.7 million acre-feet (18.1 km3), or an annualized [discharge](/wiki/Discharge_(hydrology) "Discharge (hydrology)") of 20,300 cu ft/s (570 m3/s), for the 1906–2023 period.[[70]](#cite_note-USBR_natural_flow_data-75) River flows at the Lees Ferry stream gauge, about halfway along the length of the Colorado and 16 miles (26 km) below Glen Canyon Dam, are used to determine water allocations in the Colorado River basin.[[71]](#cite_note-LeesFerry-77)[[72]](#cite_note-LeesFerryMonthly-78) Flows originating above Lees Ferry represent the majority of Colorado River runoff. Tributaries between there and [Imperial Dam](/wiki/Imperial_Dam "Imperial Dam") near the US–Mexico border contribute a further 1.1 million acre-feet (1.4 km3),[[73]](#cite_note-Schmidt_2023-79) and the Gila–[Salt River](/wiki/Salt_River_(Arizona) "Salt River (Arizona)") system historically accounted for another 1.7 million acre-feet (2.1 km3).[[74]](#cite_note-80)[[75]](#cite_note-81) While much of this water was lost to evaporation in the deserts of the Lower Basin, anywhere from 10 to 20 million acre-feet (12 to 25 km3) per year reached the Colorado River Delta prior to the early 20th century.[[76]](#cite_note-Anderson_2002-82)

Up to 90 percent of the river's flow originates as snowmelt from the Rocky Mountains and other smaller mountain ranges of the Colorado Plateau.[[77]](#cite_note-USGSflow-83) Natural seasonal flows at its mouth varied widely, often exceeding 100,000 cu ft/s (2,800 m3/s) in the spring melt and falling as low as 2,500 cu ft/s (71 m3/s) in winter.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiltshireGilbertRogers2010101–102-84) Snowmelt typically begins in April, peaks in May or June, and can extend into August in wet years.[[79]](#cite_note-85) [Monsoon](/wiki/North_American_monsoon "North American monsoon") rainstorms often occur in the Lower Basin from August through October, contributing much of the flow in tributaries below Lees Ferry and often causing [flash flooding](/wiki/Flash_flood "Flash flood").[[77]](#cite_note-USGSflow-83)[[80]](#cite_note-86) Since the initial closure of Hoover Dam in 1934, lower Colorado River flows have been greatly moderated, with monthly means below the dam ranging from 16,400 cu ft/s (460 m3/s) in May to 11,200 cu ft/s (320 m3/s) in October.[[81]](#cite_note-87) Upstream of the massive Hoover and Glen Canyon Dams, inflows to Lake Powell still experience distinct spring highs and winter lows, though overall volumes have been reduced due to Upper Basin water diversions.[[73]](#cite_note-Schmidt_2023-79) Flow regimes in a few Upper Basin tributaries, such as the [Yampa River](/wiki/Yampa_River "Yampa River"), remain almost completely unaltered.[[82]](#cite_note-88)

By the 1950s, the Colorado delta was regularly drying up in fall and winter, though spring high flows often still made it to the ocean. After the initial closure of Glen Canyon Dam in 1963, these seasonal flows were almost completely eliminated, with the exception of a few very wet years, such as 1983–1987.[[83]](#cite_note-89)[[84]](#cite_note-balance-90)[[77]](#cite_note-USGSflow-83)[[85]](#cite_note-91) In 1984, 16.5 million acre-feet (20.4 km3) of excess runoff reached the ocean.[[86]](#cite_note-92) Flows have further declined due to evaporation from reservoirs and, particularly after 2000, reduced snowpack with warming winter temperatures in the Rockies.[[87]](#cite_note-93)[[88]](#cite_note-94)[[89]](#cite_note-95) Water diversions have effectively removed some tributaries entirely, including the Gila River, once the Colorado's largest tributary below Lees Ferry.[[90]](#cite_note-96) Most Colorado River water is diverted in the United States, with only about 2.8 million acre-feet (3.5 km3) per year reaching Mexico since 1950.[[91]](#cite_note-NIB-97) At Morelos Dam, the remaining flow is diverted to irrigate the Mexicali Valley, leaving the Colorado River Delta almost entirely dry except for small amounts of agricultural wastewater.[[92]](#cite_note-98)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geology")]

As recently as the [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") period about 100 million years ago, much of western North America was still part of the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). Tectonic forces from the collision of the [Farallon Plate](/wiki/Farallon_Plate "Farallon Plate") with the [North American Plate](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate") pushed up the Rocky Mountains between 50 and 75 million years ago in a mountain-building episode known as the [Laramide orogeny](/wiki/Laramide_orogeny "Laramide orogeny").[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrisciantelli200256-106) The Colorado River first formed as a west-flowing stream draining the southwestern portion of the range, and the uplift also diverted the Green River, once a tributary of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), west towards the Colorado. About 30 to 20 million years ago, volcanic activity related to the orogeny led to the [Mid-Tertiary ignimbrite flare-up](/wiki/Mid-Tertiary_ignimbrite_flare-up "Mid-Tertiary ignimbrite flare-up"), which created smaller formations such as the [Chiricahua Mountains](/wiki/Chiricahua_Mountains "Chiricahua Mountains") in Arizona and deposited massive amounts of volcanic ash and debris over the watershed.[[100]](#cite_note-107) The Colorado Plateau first began to rise during the [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene"), between about 55 and 34 million years ago, but did not attain its present height until about 5 million years ago, about when the Colorado River established its present course into the Gulf of California.[[101]](#cite_note-108)

The time scale and sequence over which the river's present course and the Grand Canyon were formed is uncertain. Before the Gulf of California was formed around 12 to 5 million years ago by faulting processes along the boundary of the North American and [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Plate "Pacific Plate") plates,[[102]](#cite_note-109) the Colorado flowed west to an outlet on the Pacific Ocean—possibly [Monterey Bay](/wiki/Monterey_Bay "Monterey Bay") on the Central California coast, and may have played a role in the formation of the [Monterey submarine canyon](/wiki/Monterey_Canyon "Monterey Canyon"). Crustal extension in the [Basin and Range Province](/wiki/Basin_and_Range_Province "Basin and Range Province") began about 20 million years ago and the modern [Sierra Nevada](/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_(U.S.) "Sierra Nevada (U.S.)") began forming about 10 million years ago, eventually diverting the Colorado southwards towards the Gulf.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungSpamer2001193-110) As the Colorado Plateau continued to rise between 5 and 2.5 million years ago, the river maintained its ancestral course (as an [antecedent stream](/wiki/Antecedent_stream "Antecedent stream")) and began to cut the Grand Canyon. Antecedence played a major part in shaping other peculiar geographic features in the watershed, including the Dolores River's bisection of [Paradox Valley](/wiki/Paradox_Valley "Paradox Valley") in Colorado and the Green River's cut through the Uinta Mountains in Utah.[[104]](#cite_note-111)

[![View showing hardened flows of dark volcanic rock descending over the side of a canyon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/VulcanUinkart.jpg/500px-VulcanUinkart.jpg)](/wiki/File:VulcanUinkart.jpg)

Remnants of basalt flows from the [Uinkaret volcanic field](/wiki/Uinkaret_volcanic_field "Uinkaret volcanic field") are seen here descending into the Grand Canyon, where they dammed the Colorado over 10 times in the past 2 million years.

Sediments carried from the plateau by the Colorado River created a vast delta made of more than 10,000 cubic miles (42,000 km3) of material that walled off the northernmost part of the gulf in approximately 1 million years. Cut off from the ocean, the portion of the gulf north of the delta eventually evaporated and formed the [Salton Sink](/wiki/Salton_Sink "Salton Sink"), which reached about 260 feet (79 m) below sea level.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrisciantelli200262-112)[[106]](#cite_note-113) Since then the river has changed course into the Salton Sink at least three times, transforming it into [Lake Cahuilla](/wiki/Lake_Cahuilla "Lake Cahuilla"), which at maximum size flooded up the valley to present-day [Indio, California](/wiki/Indio,_California "Indio, California"). The lake took about 50 years to evaporate after the Colorado resumed flowing to the Gulf. The present-day Salton Sea can be considered the most recent incarnation of Lake Cahuilla, though on a much smaller scale.[[107]](#cite_note-114)

Between 1.8 million and 10,000 years ago, massive flows of [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") from the [Uinkaret volcanic field](/wiki/Uinkaret_volcanic_field "Uinkaret volcanic field") in northern Arizona dammed the Colorado River within the Grand Canyon. At least 13 [lava dams](/wiki/Lava_dam "Lava dam") were formed, the largest of which was more than 2,300 feet (700 m) high, backing the river up for nearly 500 miles (800 km) to present-day Moab, Utah.[[108]](#cite_note-115) The lack of associated sediment deposits along this stretch of the Colorado River, which would have accumulated in the impounded lakes over time, suggests that most of these dams did not survive for more than a few decades before collapsing or being washed away. Failure of the lava dams caused by erosion, leaks and [cavitation](/wiki/Cavitation "Cavitation") caused catastrophic floods, which may have been some of the largest ever to occur in North America, rivaling the late-[Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") [Missoula Floods](/wiki/Missoula_Floods "Missoula Floods") of the northwestern United States.[[109]](#cite_note-116) Mapping of flood deposits indicate that crests as high as 700 feet (210 m) passed through the Grand Canyon,[[110]](#cite_note-117) reaching peak discharges as great as 17 million cubic feet per second (480,000 m3/s).[[111]](#cite_note-118)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-colonial indigenous peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pre-colonial indigenous peoples")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Cliff_Palace-Colorado-Mesa_Verde_NP.jpg/330px-Cliff_Palace-Colorado-Mesa_Verde_NP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliff_Palace-Colorado-Mesa_Verde_NP.jpg)

Cliff Palace, [Mesa Verde National Park](/wiki/Mesa_Verde_National_Park "Mesa Verde National Park")

Small numbers of [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indian "Paleo-Indian") of the [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") and [Folsom cultures](/wiki/Folsom_culture "Folsom culture") inhabited the Colorado Plateau as early as 10,000 BCE, with populations beginning to increase in the Desert Archaic period (6000 BCE–0 CE).[[112]](#cite_note-CP-119) While most early inhabitants were hunter-gatherers, evidence of agriculture, masonry dwellings and [petroglyphs](/wiki/Petroglyph "Petroglyph") begins with the [Fremont culture](/wiki/Fremont_culture "Fremont culture") period (0–1300 CE). The [Ancient Puebloan](/wiki/Ancient_Puebloan "Ancient Puebloan") culture, also known as Anasazi or Hisatsinom, were descended from the Desert Archaic culture and became established in the Four Corners region around 1000 CE.[[113]](#cite_note-120)[[114]](#cite_note-121)[[115]](#cite_note-122)

While there is much evidence of ancient habitation along the Colorado River, including stone dwellings, petroglyphs and pottery in places such as Glen Canyon,[[116]](#cite_note-123) the first major agriculture-based societies arose a significant distance from the river. The [Puebloan](/wiki/Puebloans "Puebloans") people built many multi-story pueblos or "great houses", and developed complex distribution systems to supply drinking and irrigation water in [Chaco Canyon](/wiki/Chaco_Canyon "Chaco Canyon") in northwestern New Mexico[[117]](#cite_note-luhna-124) and [Mesa Verde](/wiki/Mesa_Verde_National_Park "Mesa Verde National Park") in southwest Colorado.[[118]](#cite_note-125) The [Hohokam](/wiki/Hohokam "Hohokam"), present in the modern Phoenix area since about 0 CE, experienced prolific growth around 600–700 CE as they constructed a large system of irrigation canals making use of the [Salt River](/wiki/Salt_River_(Arizona) "Salt River (Arizona)"). Both civilizations supported large populations at their height, with 6,000–15,000 in Chaco Canyon[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENobles199826-126) and as many as 30,000–200,000 Hohokam.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELogan200621–22-127)

|  |
| --- |
| **Indigenous names for the Colorado River** |
| [Hopi](/wiki/Hopi_language "Hopi language"): *Pisisvayu*[[121]](#cite_note-128) |
| [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_language "Maricopa language"): *'Xakxwet*[[122]](#cite_note-129) |
| [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language"): *'Aha Kwahwat*[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta2010362-130) |
| [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_language "Navajo language"): *Tó Ntsʼósíkooh* |
| [Havasupai](/wiki/Havasupai_language "Havasupai language"): *Ha Ŧay Gʼam* / *Sil Gsvgov*[[124]](#cite_note-131) |
| [Yavapai](/wiki/Yavapai_language "Yavapai language"): *ʼHakhwata*[[125]](#cite_note-132) |

Puebloan and Hohokam settlements were abruptly abandoned in the 1400s CE, due both to over-exploitation of natural resources such as timber, and severe drought that made it impossible to maintain irrigation systems.[[126]](#cite_note-tenthmil-133)[[127]](#cite_note-Schwinning-134) Many Puebloans migrated east to the Rio Grande Valley, while others persisted in smaller settlements on the Colorado Plateau. Puebloan descendants include the [Hopi](/wiki/Hopi "Hopi"), [Zuni](/wiki/Zuni_people "Zuni people"), [Laguna](/wiki/Laguna_people "Laguna people") and [Acoma](/wiki/Acoma_people "Acoma people") peoples of modern Arizona and New Mexico.[[112]](#cite_note-CP-119) [O'odham](/wiki/O%27odham "O'odham") peoples, including the [Akimel O'odham](/wiki/Akimel_O%27odham "Akimel O'odham") (Pima) and [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_people "Maricopa people") who continue to live in southern Arizona, are believed to be descended from the Hohokam.[[112]](#cite_note-CP-119)[[128]](#cite_note-135)[[129]](#cite_note-pima-136)

The lower Colorado River valley was inhabited for thousands of years by numerous tribes of the [Patayan](/wiki/Patayan "Patayan") cultures, many of which belong to the [Yuman-Cochimi language group](/wiki/Yuman-Cochimi_languages "Yuman-Cochimi languages"). These include the [Walapai](/wiki/Walapai "Walapai"), [Havasupai](/wiki/Havasupai "Havasupai") and [Yavapai](/wiki/Yavapai "Yavapai") in the Grand Canyon region; the [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people"), [Halchidhoma](/wiki/Halchidhoma "Halchidhoma"), [Quechan](/wiki/Quechan_people "Quechan people"), and [Halyikwamai](/wiki/Halyikwamai "Halyikwamai") along the Colorado River between Black Canyon and the Mexican border, and the [Cocopah](/wiki/Cocopah_people "Cocopah people") around the Colorado River Delta. The [Chemehuevi](/wiki/Chemehuevi "Chemehuevi") (a branch of the [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute")) and the [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay") inhabited the desert to the river's west.[[130]](#cite_note-Yuman-137) Those living along the lower Colorado River depended more on fishing and floodplain agriculture than on irrigation, and mostly did not live in permanent settlements.[[131]](#cite_note-138) The site of modern-day Yuma has been [an important river crossing](/wiki/Yuma_Crossing "Yuma Crossing") since ancient times, as the channel here is much narrower compared to the expansive, swampy river bottoms to the north and south, and enabled the expansion of trade to the Pima and Maricopa in the east and coastal California tribes in the west.[[130]](#cite_note-Yuman-137)

[![Black and white photograph of a Native American woman holding a child](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Navajo_Woman_and_Infant%2C_Canyon_de_Chelle%2C_Arizona_%28Canyon_de_Chelly_National_Monument%29%2C_1933_-_1942_-_NARA_-_519947.jpg/250px-Navajo_Woman_and_Infant%2C_Canyon_de_Chelle%2C_Arizona_%28Canyon_de_Chelly_National_Monument%29%2C_1933_-_1942_-_NARA_-_519947.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navajo_Woman_and_Infant,_Canyon_de_Chelle,_Arizona_(Canyon_de_Chelly_National_Monument),_1933_-_1942_-_NARA_-_519947.jpg)

[Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_people "Navajo people") woman and child, photographed by [Ansel Adams](/wiki/Ansel_Adams "Ansel Adams"), c. 1944

The [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_people "Navajo people") (Diné) began migrating into the Colorado River Basin around 1000–1500 CE, and eventually exercised influence over much of the Colorado Plateau. Originally hunter-gatherers, they acquired knowledge of farming from the Puebloans and adopted a more sedentary lifestyle over time, making extensive use of irrigation in their settlements.[[132]](#cite_note-NavajoUC-139)[[133]](#cite_note-140) The Navajo gradually displaced Hopi settlements as they expanded into northern Arizona after the 1500s. [Navajo Mountain](/wiki/Navajo_Mountain "Navajo Mountain") and [Rainbow Bridge](/wiki/Rainbow_Bridge_National_Monument "Rainbow Bridge National Monument") in the Glen Canyon area came to hold particular religious significance for the Navajo, and the nearby confluence of the Colorado and San Juan River is regarded as the birthplace of clouds and rain.[[134]](#cite_note-141)

The [Ute](/wiki/Ute_people "Ute people") also became established in the Colorado Plateau around 1500 CE, although they had inhabited more northerly parts of the Colorado basin (modern Wyoming and northern Colorado) since at least 0 CE.[[135]](#cite_note-142)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005486-143) They are the first known inhabitants of this part of the Rocky Mountains, and made use of an extensive network of trails crisscrossing the mountains to move between summer and winter camps. The Ute were divided into numerous bands with separate territories but shared a common language and customs. The Uncompahgre or Tabeguache lived around the confluence of the upper Colorado and Gunnison Rivers, an area including the [Grand Mesa](/wiki/Grand_Mesa "Grand Mesa"); the Weenuchiu lived along the San Juan River, and the Parianuche and Yamparika lived in the Yampa, [White](/wiki/White_River_(Green_River_tributary) "White River (Green River tributary)") and [Duchesne River](/wiki/Duchesne_River "Duchesne River") valleys.[[137]](#cite_note-144) The Ute ranged as far as the river's headwaters; one Ute story recounts a battle with the [Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho_people "Arapaho people") at Grand Lake, which they believe still hosts the spirits of the deceased.

### Spanish exploration and early settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Spanish exploration and early settlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg/250px-La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg)

*La conquista del Colorado* (2017), by [Augusto Ferrer-Dalmau](/wiki/Augusto_Ferrer-Dalmau "Augusto Ferrer-Dalmau"), depicts [Francisco Vázquez de Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1zquez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vázquez de Coronado")'s 1540–1542 expedition. [García López de Cárdenas](/wiki/Garc%C3%ADa_L%C3%B3pez_de_C%C3%A1rdenas "García López de Cárdenas") can be seen overlooking the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon").

Starting in the 1500s, the Spanish began to explore and colonize western North America. [Francisco de Ulloa](/wiki/Francisco_de_Ulloa "Francisco de Ulloa") may have been the first European to see the river, when in 1536 he sailed to the head of the Gulf of California.[[138]](#cite_note-145) In 1540 [García López de Cárdenas](/wiki/Garc%C3%ADa_L%C3%B3pez_de_C%C3%A1rdenas "García López de Cárdenas") became the first European to see the Grand Canyon, during [Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1squez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vásquez de Coronado")'s expedition to find the [Seven Cities of Gold](/wiki/Seven_Cities_of_Gold "Seven Cities of Gold") ("Cibola"). Cárdenas was apparently unimpressed with the canyon, greatly underestimating its size, and left in disappointment with no gold to be found.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxelrodPhillips20084-146)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELankford2010100–101-147) In the same year [Melchior Díaz](/wiki/Melchior_D%C3%ADaz "Melchior Díaz") explored the Colorado River's delta and named it *Rio del Tizon* ("fire brand river"), after seeing a practice used by the local people for warming themselves.[[141]](#cite_note-148)

By the late 1500s or early 1600s, the Utes had acquired horses from the Spanish, and their use for hunting, trade and warfare soon became widespread among Utes and Navajo in the Colorado River basin. This conferred them a military advantage over [Goshutes](/wiki/Goshute "Goshute") and [Southern Paiutes](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute") that were slower to adopt horses.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPritzker1998309-149) The Navajo also adopted a culture of livestock herding as they acquired sheep and goats from the Spanish.[[132]](#cite_note-NavajoUC-139) [Juan Bautista de Anza](/wiki/Juan_Bautista_de_Anza "Juan Bautista de Anza") in 1774 was the first Spaniard to reach Yuma Crossing, where he established friendly relations with the Quechan people and opened the [Anza trail](/wiki/Juan_Bautista_de_Anza_National_Historic_Trail "Juan Bautista de Anza National Historic Trail") between Arizona and the California coast.[[143]](#cite_note-YumaCrossingNPS-150) The Spanish soon founded [Mission Puerto de Purisima Concepcion](/wiki/Mission_Puerto_de_Purisima_Concepcion "Mission Puerto de Purisima Concepcion") and [Mission San Pedro y San Pablo de Bicuner](/wiki/Mission_San_Pedro_y_San_Pablo_de_Bicuner "Mission San Pedro y San Pablo de Bicuner") along the lower Colorado River. However, Spanish attempts to control the crossing led to the 1781 Yuma revolt, in which over 100 soldiers and colonists were killed, and the settlements were abandoned. The Quechan blocked foreigners' use of the crossing until the arrival of American mountain men and fur trappers in the 1820s.[[143]](#cite_note-YumaCrossingNPS-150)

The name *Rio Colorado* first appears in 1701, on the map "Paso por Tierra a la California" published by missionary [Eusebio Kino](/wiki/Eusebio_Kino "Eusebio Kino"), who also determined during that time that Baja California was a peninsula, not an island as previously believed.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBolton2017440-151) In the 1700s and early 1800s many Spanish and American explorers believed in the existence of a [Buenaventura River](/wiki/Buenaventura_River_(legend) "Buenaventura River (legend)") that ran from the Rocky Mountains to the Pacific coast.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuddeBright200450-152) In 1776, [Silvestre Vélez de Escalante](/wiki/Silvestre_V%C3%A9lez_de_Escalante "Silvestre Vélez de Escalante") attached this name to the upper Green River, and a number of later maps showed this connecting to Lake Timpanogos (now [Utah Lake](/wiki/Utah_Lake "Utah Lake")) and flowing west to California. The [Dominguez–Escalante expedition](/wiki/Dominguez%E2%80%93Escalante_expedition "Dominguez–Escalante expedition") first reached the Colorado River near the junction with the [Dolores River](/wiki/Dolores_River "Dolores River"), naming the larger river "Rio San Rafael". They later forded the Colorado in southeastern Utah at [Crossing of the Fathers](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Fathers "Crossing of the Fathers"), now submerged in Lake Powell.[[146]](#cite_note-153)

### American exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: American exploration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/%27Noon_Day_Rest_in_Marble_Canyon%27_from_the_second_Powell_Expedition_1872.jpg/250px-%27Noon_Day_Rest_in_Marble_Canyon%27_from_the_second_Powell_Expedition_1872.jpg)](/wiki/File:%27Noon_Day_Rest_in_Marble_Canyon%27_from_the_second_Powell_Expedition_1872.jpg)

Boats of [John Wesley Powell](/wiki/John_Wesley_Powell "John Wesley Powell")'s second Colorado River expedition in [Marble Canyon](/wiki/Marble_Canyon "Marble Canyon"), 1872.

In the 1820s, American fur trappers along the upper Green River in Wyoming (known to them as the "Seedskeedee" or variants thereof), seeking a route to export furs to the coast, surmised that this and what the Spanish called the Colorado were in fact connected. [William H. Ashley](/wiki/William_H._Ashley "William H. Ashley") made an unsuccessful attempt to navigate from the Green River to the Colorado's mouth in 1825.[[147]](#cite_note-Before_Powell-154) In 1826, [Jedediah Smith](/wiki/Jedediah_Smith "Jedediah Smith") arrived at the lower Colorado River, referring to it as the Seedskeedee,[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDellenbaugh190960-155) and proceeded upstream, exploring as far as Black Canyon.[[147]](#cite_note-Before_Powell-154) During the 1830s, various fur trappers from Wyoming made it as far downstream as Cataract Canyon and Glen Canyon, but none were able to navigate the full length of the river.[[147]](#cite_note-Before_Powell-154) In 1843 [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont") explored the Great Basin and conclusively determined no Buenaventura River flowed west to California; thus, the direction of river flow must be southwest.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERolle199954-156)

By the early 19th century, the stretch of the Colorado above the confluence of the Green River at [Cataract Canyon](/wiki/Cataract_Canyon "Cataract Canyon"), Utah, became known to fur trappers as the "Grand River", though the exact origin of this name is unknown. The Grand River above the confluence with the [Gunnison River](/wiki/Gunnison_River "Gunnison River") was also called the Bunkara River, the Blue River, or the North Fork of the Grand River until the 1870s.[[150]](#cite_note-157)[[151]](#cite_note-congress-158) By the early 1900s the name "Grand River" had been attached to the entire stream as far as Grand Lake, which was then considered its official source.[[152]](#cite_note-159) Although the Grand River was renamed the Colorado in 1921, its name survives in numerous places such as Grand County and Grand Junction, Colorado.[[153]](#cite_note-160)

In 1848 the U.S. Army established [Fort Yuma](/wiki/Fort_Yuma "Fort Yuma"), creating the first permanent U.S. settlement along the river. This served as a military garrison and supply point for settlers headed to California along the [Southern Emigrant Trail](/wiki/Southern_Emigrant_Trail "Southern Emigrant Trail"). Due to the arduous task of ferrying supplies overland, the schooner *[Invincible](/wiki/Invincible_(schooner) "Invincible (schooner)")* attempted to bring supplies up the river but was thwarted by the delta's strong tides. Steamboats were brought to the river, starting in 1852 with the sidewheeler *[Uncle Sam](/wiki/Uncle_Sam_(sidewheeler_1852) "Uncle Sam (sidewheeler 1852)")*, whose first voyage from the Gulf to Yuma took fifteen days.[[154]](#cite_note-Marcucci-161): 15 Exploration by steamboat soon advanced upriver. In 1857, George A. Johnson in the *[General Jesup](/wiki/General_Jesup_(sidewheeler) "General Jesup (sidewheeler)")* was able to reach [Pyramid Canyon](/wiki/Pyramid_Canyon "Pyramid Canyon"), over 300 miles (480 km) north of Fort Yuma.[[155]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-162): 16–17, 19[[156]](#cite_note-163) He was followed by Lt. [Joseph Christmas Ives](/wiki/Joseph_Christmas_Ives "Joseph Christmas Ives") who used a specially built shallow-draft steamboat, *[Explorer](/wiki/Explorer_(sternwheeler) "Explorer (sternwheeler)")*, to reach [Black Canyon](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Colorado "Black Canyon of the Colorado"), where Hoover Dam stands today.[[157]](#cite_note-Ives-164): Part 1, 85–87 Having set out to determine the river's suitability as a navigation route, Ives remarked: "Ours has been the first, and will doubtless be the last, party of whites to visit this profitless locality. It seems intended by nature that the Colorado River, along the greater portion of its lonely and majestic way, shall be forever unvisited and undisturbed."[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDellenbaugh1909170-165)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidt199312-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/First_Powell_Expedition_1869_USGS_Pioh120.jpg/250px-First_Powell_Expedition_1869_USGS_Pioh120.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Powell_Expedition_1869_USGS_Pioh120.jpg)

Route of Powell's first expedition, 1869.

The last part of the Colorado River to be surveyed was the Grand Canyon itself. In 1869, [John Wesley Powell](/wiki/John_Wesley_Powell "John Wesley Powell") with nine men set out on [an expedition](/wiki/Powell_Geographic_Expedition_of_1869 "Powell Geographic Expedition of 1869") from [Green River Station, Wyoming](/wiki/Green_River,_Wyoming "Green River, Wyoming"). They were the first party of non-natives to travel the length of the Grand Canyon, and the first to successfully travel by boat from the upper Green River to the lower Colorado.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDolnick20025-167)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2000360-168) Powell led a second expedition in 1871, with financial backing from the U.S. government,[[162]](#cite_note-169) and continued to conduct geographical and botanical surveys across the region until the 1890s.[[163]](#cite_note-170) Another Grand Canyon river expedition was led in 1889–1890 by [Robert Brewster Stanton](/wiki/Robert_Brewster_Stanton "Robert Brewster Stanton") to survey a route for a proposed railroad through the canyon, which was never built.[[164]](#cite_note-171)

> We are now ready to start on our way down the Great Unknown. Our boats, tied to a common stake, are chafing each other, as they are tossed by the fretful river. They ride high and buoyant, for their loads are lighter than we could desire. We have but a month's rations remaining… The lighting of the boats has this advantage: they will ride the waves better, and we shall have little to carry when we make a portage.
>
> We are three-quarters of a mile in the depths of the earth, and the great river shrinks into insignificance, as it dashes its angry waves against the walls and cliffs, that rise to the world above; they are but puny ripples, and we but pigmies, running up and down the sands, or lost among the boulders.
>
> We have an unknown distance yet to run; an unknown river yet to explore. What falls there are, we know not; what rocks beset the channel, we know not; what walls rise over the river, we know not; Ah, well! we may conjecture many things. The men talk as cheerfully as ever; jests are bandied about freely this morning; but to me the cheer is somber and the jests are ghastly.

— John Wesley Powell's journal, August 1869[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDolnick2002238-172)

### U.S. westward expansion and military campaigns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: U.S. westward expansion and military campaigns")]

See also: [Steamboats of the Colorado River](/wiki/Steamboats_of_the_Colorado_River "Steamboats of the Colorado River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Fort_Yuma_California_1875.jpg/250px-Fort_Yuma_California_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Yuma_California_1875.jpg)

[Lithograph](/wiki/Lithograph "Lithograph") of Fort Yuma, c. 1875

In 1858, gold was discovered on the Gila River east of Yuma, then along the Colorado River at [El Dorado Canyon, Nevada](/wiki/El_Dorado_Canyon_(Nevada) "El Dorado Canyon (Nevada)") and [La Paz, Arizona](/wiki/La_Paz,_Arizona "La Paz, Arizona").[[166]](#cite_note-173)[[167]](#cite_note-174) As prospectors and settlers entered the region, they became involved in skirmishes with the Mohave, spurring U.S. Army expeditions that culminated in the 1859 Battle of the Colorado River which concluded the [Mohave War](/wiki/Mohave_War "Mohave War").[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKesselWooster2005217-175) In the 1870s the Mohave were moved to the [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mojave_Indian_Reservation "Fort Mojave Indian Reservation") and [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation "Colorado River Indian Reservation") reservations.[[169]](#cite_note-176)[[170]](#cite_note-CRIT-177) Chemehuevi and later some Hopi and Navajo peoples were also moved to the Colorado River reservation, where they today form the [Colorado River Indian Tribes](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes").[[171]](#cite_note-178)

As the American frontier expanded into the Colorado Plateau, an effort to expel the Navajo from the Four Corners region was begun by General [James Henry Carleton](/wiki/James_Henry_Carleton "James Henry Carleton"), who in 1864 enlisted mountain man [Kit Carson](/wiki/Kit_Carson "Kit Carson") to lead a campaign against the Navajo. Carson, with the help of the Navajo's Ute enemies, captured more than 8,000 Navajo and forcibly marched them to [Fort Sumner](/wiki/Fort_Sumner "Fort Sumner"), New Mexico. Hundreds died during what is now known as the [Long Walk](/wiki/Long_Walk_of_the_Navajo "Long Walk of the Navajo") and while enduring appalling conditions at Fort Sumner. After the failure of the Army to maintain the reservation there, the [Treaty of Bosque Redondo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bosque_Redondo "Treaty of Bosque Redondo") established the [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") in the Four Corners, where the Navajo were allowed to return in 1868.[[172]](#cite_note-179)[[173]](#cite_note-180)[[174]](#cite_note-181)

Gold and silver were also discovered in the Upper Basin, beginning with the 1859 Blue River strike that led to the founding of [Breckenridge, Colorado](/wiki/Breckenridge,_Colorado "Breckenridge, Colorado").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown197252–53-182) Up until the 1860s, southwestern Colorado had remained relatively untouched by U.S. westward expansion, as the Americans had recognized Ute sovereignty by treaty. Following the 1861 carving out of [Colorado Territory](/wiki/Colorado_Territory "Colorado Territory") and further mineral strikes including [Ouray](/wiki/Ouray,_Colorado "Ouray, Colorado") and [Telluride](/wiki/Telluride,_Colorado "Telluride, Colorado"),[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasey2007251-183)[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg2009134–135-184) Ute leaders were coerced into signing the 1873 [Brunot Agreement](/wiki/Brunot_Agreement "Brunot Agreement"), in which they lost rights to most of their land. A flood of mineral prospecting and settlement ensued in western Colorado.[[178]](#cite_note-185) By 1881, the Army had driven out the remaining pockets of Ute resistance on the Western Slope, officially opening the Grand River country to settlement, and the town of Grand Junction was incorporated a year later.[[179]](#cite_note-186) The [Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad](/wiki/Denver_and_Rio_Grande_Western_Railroad "Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad") (D&RGW) quickly expanded into this area to serve mining boomtowns, crossing the Rockies to the south via the [Black Canyon of the Gunnison River](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Gunnison_River "Black Canyon of the Gunnison River"). By 1883 the railroad had reached Grand Junction, and a spur up the Colorado to Glenwood Springs was completed in 1887.[[180]](#cite_note-187)

In [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Territory "Arizona Territory") and [Utah Territories](/wiki/Utah_Territory "Utah Territory"), many early settlers were Mormons fleeing religious persecution in the Midwest. Mormons founded agricultural colonies at Fort Santa Clara in 1855 and [St. Thomas](/wiki/St._Thomas,_Nevada "St. Thomas, Nevada"), now flooded under Lake Mead, in 1865.[[181]](#cite_note-188) [Stone's Ferry](/wiki/Stone%27s_Ferry,_Nevada "Stone's Ferry, Nevada"), crossing the Colorado at the mouth of the Virgin River, enabled shipping of their produce by wagon to gold mining districts further south. Although the Mormons abandoned St. Thomas in 1871, a salt-[mining industry](/wiki/Mining_industry "Mining industry") persisted here, and steamboats operated up to nearby [Rioville](/wiki/Rioville,_Nevada "Rioville, Nevada") into the 1880s.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGlassGlass1983162–163-189)[[155]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-162): 78 In 1879 [a group of Mormon settlers](/wiki/San_Juan_Expedition "San Juan Expedition") made their way to southeastern Utah, blasting the precarious [Hole in the Rock Trail](/wiki/Hole_in_the_Rock_Trail "Hole in the Rock Trail") to cross the Colorado River at Glen Canyon, subsequently establishing the community of [Bluff](/wiki/Bluff,_Utah "Bluff, Utah").[[183]](#cite_note-190) Due to the dry climate, these settlements depended heavily on irrigation. In central Arizona, settlers uncovered and re-established canals previously used by the Hohokam.[[184]](#cite_note-hohokam-191)[[185]](#cite_note-Gilavalley-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/File-Historic_photograph_of_ferryboat_at_Lees_Ferry._John-D._Lee_established_the_first_ferry_at_the_confluence_of_the-Colorado_and_%28698b6643-1dc1-4738-866e-a38aa7046253%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:File-Historic_photograph_of_ferryboat_at_Lees_Ferry._John-D._Lee_established_the_first_ferry_at_the_confluence_of_the-Colorado_and_(698b6643-1dc1-4738-866e-a38aa7046253).jpg)

Historic photograph of the cable ferry at Lee's Ferry, prior to construction of the Navajo Bridge.

Following tensions between Mormon settlers and the U.S. government in the [Utah War](/wiki/Utah_War "Utah War"), a [local militia](/wiki/Nauvoo_Legion "Nauvoo Legion") including [John D. Lee](/wiki/John_D._Lee "John D. Lee") perpetrated the 1857 [Mountain Meadows Massacre](/wiki/Mountain_Meadows_Massacre "Mountain Meadows Massacre"), in which 120 non-Mormon settlers were killed. Fearing retribution, Lee moved in 1870 to the remote Pahreah Crossing in Arizona, where he took over a ferry first established in 1864 by [Jacob Hamblin](/wiki/Jacob_Hamblin "Jacob Hamblin").[[186]](#cite_note-GCNleesferry-193) This, the only river crossing for hundreds of miles not hemmed in by vertical canyon walls, became known as [Lee's Ferry](/wiki/Lee%27s_Ferry "Lee's Ferry"). While Lee was tried and subsequently executed in 1877, the ferry remained a major transportation link until the [Navajo Bridge](/wiki/Navajo_Bridge "Navajo Bridge") was completed nearby in 1928, rendering the ferry obsolete.[[187]](#cite_note-194)

The [Denver and Salt Lake Railway](/wiki/Denver_and_Salt_Lake_Railway "Denver and Salt Lake Railway") (D&SL), incorporated in 1902, sought to provide a more direct connection between Denver and [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City,_Utah "Salt Lake City, Utah") than either the [transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_Transcontinental_Railroad "First Transcontinental Railroad") through Wyoming or the D&RGW's route via Black Canyon and Durango. The D&SL completed a rail line into the upper headwaters of the Colorado River and blasted the [Moffat Tunnel](/wiki/Moffat_Tunnel "Moffat Tunnel") under the Continental Divide, but ran out of money before even reaching Utah.[[188]](#cite_note-MoffatRoad-195) In 1931 the D&RGW completed the "Dotsero Cutoff" linking Glenwood Springs to the D&SL route at [Bond, Colorado](/wiki/Bond,_Colorado "Bond, Colorado"), finally completing the direct Denver–Salt Lake link with its acquisition of the bankrupt D&SL. The Gunnison River route was eventually abandoned in favor of the shorter Colorado River route, which today is owned by [Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad").[[188]](#cite_note-MoffatRoad-195)[[189]](#cite_note-196)

### Renaming of the upper Colorado River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Renaming of the upper Colorado River")]

As late as 1921, the Colorado River upstream from the confluence with the Green River in Utah was still known as the Grand River. For over a decade, U.S. Representative [Edward T. Taylor](/wiki/Edward_T._Taylor "Edward T. Taylor") of Colorado had petitioned the [Congressional Committee on Interstate and Foreign Commerce](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Energy_and_Commerce "United States House Committee on Energy and Commerce") to rename the Grand River as the Colorado River.[[151]](#cite_note-congress-158)[[190]](#cite_note-197) Representatives from Wyoming, Utah, and the [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") objected, noting that the Green River was longer and drained a larger area. Taylor argued that the Grand River should be considered the main stream, as it carried the larger volume of water.[[151]](#cite_note-congress-158)[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes1988104-198)[[n 8]](#cite_note-200) Taylor felt "slighted" that the Colorado River, as named, did not begin in the state of Colorado, and "he wasn't going to let Utah or Wyoming lay claim to the river's headwaters, despite the fact that the Green River is the larger drainage basin."[[193]](#cite_note-201) On July 25, 1921, [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Warren G. Harding](/wiki/Warren_G._Harding "Warren G. Harding") signed House Joint Resolution 32 - To change the name of the Grand River in Colorado and Utah to the Colorado River.[[194]](#cite_note-Grand_River_renamed-202)

## Engineering and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Engineering and development")]

[![Front view of a dam in a narrow canyon, with water shooting out of the gates](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/HooverDamFrontWater.jpg/250px-HooverDamFrontWater.jpg)](/wiki/File:HooverDamFrontWater.jpg)

[Hoover Dam](/wiki/Hoover_Dam "Hoover Dam") releasing water in 1998

See also: [International Boundary and Water Commission](/wiki/International_Boundary_and_Water_Commission "International Boundary and Water Commission"), [List of dams in the Colorado River system](/wiki/List_of_dams_in_the_Colorado_River_system "List of dams in the Colorado River system"), and [Colorado River Compact](/wiki/Colorado_River_Compact "Colorado River Compact")

About 40 million people depend on the Colorado River's water for agricultural, industrial and domestic needs.[[195]](#cite_note-Water_Supply_and_Demand_Study-203) The Colorado irrigates 5.5 million acres (2.2 million hectares) of farmland,[[195]](#cite_note-Water_Supply_and_Demand_Study-203) and its hydroelectric plants produce 12 billion [kilowatt hours](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour "Kilowatt hour") (KWh) of hydroelectricity each year.[[196]](#cite_note-Controversy-204) Hydroelectricity from the Colorado is a key supplier of [peaking power](/wiki/Peaking_power "Peaking power") on the Southwest electric grid.[[197]](#cite_note-205)[[198]](#cite_note-206) Often called "America's [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile")",[[199]](#cite_note-207) the Colorado River is so intensively managed that each drop of its water is used an average of 17 times in a single year.[[200]](#cite_note-208)[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReisner1993120-209) [Southern Nevada Water Authority](/wiki/Southern_Nevada_Water_Authority "Southern Nevada Water Authority") has called the Colorado River one of the "most controlled, controversial and litigated rivers in the world".[[202]](#cite_note-210)

| Colorado River water allocations, in millions of [acre-feet](/wiki/Acre-foot "Acre-foot")[[203]](#cite_note-CRCtext-211)[[204]](#cite_note-1944Treaty-212)[[205]](#cite_note-213) | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| User | Amount | Share |
| **United States** | **15.0** | **90.9%** |
| California | 4.4 | 26.7% |
| Colorado | 3.88 | 23.5% |
| Arizona | 2.8 | 17.0% |
| Utah | 1.72 | 10.4% |
| Wyoming | 1.05 | 6.4% |
| New Mexico | 0.84 | 5.1% |
| Nevada | 0.3 | 1.8% |
| **Mexico** | **1.5** | **9.1%** |
| **Total** | **16.5** | **100%** |

In 1922, six U.S. states signed the [Colorado River Compact](/wiki/Colorado_River_Compact "Colorado River Compact"), which divided half of the river's flow to both the Upper Basin (the drainage area above Lee's Ferry, comprising parts of Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, and Wyoming and a small portion of Arizona) and the Lower Basin (Arizona, California, Nevada, and parts of New Mexico and Utah). The Upper and Lower Basin were each allocated 7.5 million acre-feet (9.3 km3) of water per year, a figure believed to represent half of the river's annual flow at Lee's Ferry.[[203]](#cite_note-CRCtext-211) The allotments operated under the premise that approximately 17.5 million acre-feet of water flowed through the river annually.[[206]](#cite_note-214)

Arizona initially refused to ratify the compact because it feared that California would take too much of the lower basin allotment.
