In 1944 a compromise was reached in which Arizona was allocated 2.8 million acre-feet (3.5 km3), but with the caveat that California's 4.4-million-acre-foot (5.4 km3) allocation was prioritized during drought years.[[207]](#cite_note-UtahP-215) These and nine other decisions, compacts, federal acts and agreements made between 1922 and 1973 constitute what is now known as the Law of the River.[[207]](#cite_note-UtahP-215)[[208]](#cite_note-LoR-216)

In 1944, [a treaty between the U.S. and Mexico](/wiki/International_Boundary_and_Water_Commission#1944_Water_Treaty "International Boundary and Water Commission") allocated 1.5 million acre-feet (1.9 km3) of Colorado River water to Mexico each year.[[204]](#cite_note-1944Treaty-212) [Morelos Dam](/wiki/Morelos_Dam "Morelos Dam") was constructed in 1950 to enable Mexico to utilize its share of the river. Water allocated to Mexico from the Colorado River is regulated by the [International Boundary and Water Commission](/wiki/International_Boundary_and_Water_Commission "International Boundary and Water Commission"), which also apportions waters from the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") between the two countries.[[209]](#cite_note-217)

### Transmountain diversions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Transmountain diversions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/GRAND_DITCH_OVERVIEW%2C_VIEWING_SOUTH_-_Grand_Ditch%2C_Baker_Creek_to_LaPoudre_Pass_Creek%2C_Grand_Lake%2C_Grand_County%2C_CO_HAER_COLO%2C25-GRLK.V%2C2-1.tif/lossy-page1-250px-GRAND_DITCH_OVERVIEW%2C_VIEWING_SOUTH_-_Grand_Ditch%2C_Baker_Creek_to_LaPoudre_Pass_Creek%2C_Grand_Lake%2C_Grand_County%2C_CO_HAER_COLO%2C25-GRLK.V%2C2-1.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:GRAND_DITCH_OVERVIEW,_VIEWING_SOUTH_-_Grand_Ditch,_Baker_Creek_to_LaPoudre_Pass_Creek,_Grand_Lake,_Grand_County,_CO_HAER_COLO,25-GRLK.V,2-1.tif)

The Grand Ditch, one of the earliest water diversions of the Colorado River, is still in use today.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Little_Oso_Diversion_Dam.jpg/250px-Little_Oso_Diversion_Dam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Little_Oso_Diversion_Dam.jpg)

The Little Oso Diversion Dam diverts water from the [Little Navajo River](/wiki/Little_Navajo_River "Little Navajo River") and transports it through a series of tunnels into the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") basin as part of the [San Juan–Chama Project](/wiki/San_Juan%E2%80%93Chama_Project "San Juan–Chama Project").

Large-scale development of Colorado River water supplies started in the late 19th century, at the river's headwaters in La Poudre Pass. The [Grand Ditch](/wiki/Grand_Ditch "Grand Ditch"), directing runoff from the river's headwaters across the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide "Continental Divide") to arid eastern Colorado, was considered an engineering marvel when completed in 1890.[[210]](#cite_note-218) This was the first of twenty-four "transmountain diversions" constructed to draw water across the Rocky Mountains as the [Front Range corridor](/wiki/Front_Range_urban_corridor "Front Range urban corridor") increased in population.[[211]](#cite_note-219) These diversions draw water from the upper Colorado and its tributaries into the [South Platte River](/wiki/South_Platte_River "South Platte River"), [Arkansas River](/wiki/Arkansas_River "Arkansas River") and [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande") basins.[[212]](#cite_note-Water_Education_CO-220) Today, about 80 percent of Colorado's population lives on the eastern slope of the Rockies, while 80 percent of precipitation falls on the western slope.[[213]](#cite_note-221)

While first planned at the same time as the Grand Ditch, construction on the [Colorado–Big Thompson Project](/wiki/Colorado%E2%80%93Big_Thompson_Project "Colorado–Big Thompson Project") (C-BT) did not begin until the 1930s. Today, the C-BT is the largest of the transmountain diversions, delivering 230,000 acre-feet (280,000,000 m3) per year from the Colorado River to cities north of [Denver](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado").[[214]](#cite_note-222) Numerous other projects followed, with the largest including the Roberts Tunnel, which delivers water from the [Blue River](/wiki/Blue_River_(Colorado) "Blue River (Colorado)") to the city of Denver,[[215]](#cite_note-223)[[216]](#cite_note-224) and the [Fryingpan–Arkansas Project](/wiki/Fryingpan%E2%80%93Arkansas_Project "Fryingpan–Arkansas Project"), which diverts water from the [Fryingpan River](/wiki/Fryingpan_River "Fryingpan River") to the [Arkansas River](/wiki/Arkansas_River "Arkansas River") basin.[[217]](#cite_note-225)

Combined, the transmountain diversions draw about 580,000 acre-feet (720,000,000 m3) of water per year out of the Colorado River basin.[[212]](#cite_note-Water_Education_CO-220) Historically, most of the water has been used for irrigation, although water usage is increasing for urban water supply and for recreational purposes such as [snowmaking](/wiki/Snowmaking "Snowmaking") and increasing Eastern Slope streamflows for boating and fishing. [Denver Water](/wiki/Denver_Water "Denver Water") receives about 50 percent of its supply from the Colorado River basin. However, diversions have caused environmental harm to the upper Colorado River system by reducing streamflows in many tributaries.[[212]](#cite_note-Water_Education_CO-220) A number of reservoirs have been built to offset the impact of transmountain diversions by storing water for dry season release on the Western Slope, including [Williams Fork Reservoir](/wiki/Williams_Fork_Reservoir "Williams Fork Reservoir") in 1959[[218]](#cite_note-226) and [Wolford Mountain Reservoir](/wiki/Wolford_Mountain_Reservoir "Wolford Mountain Reservoir") in 1996.[[219]](#cite_note-227)

### The Imperial Valley and the Salton Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Imperial Valley and the Salton Sea")]

Main articles: [Alamo Canal](/wiki/Alamo_Canal "Alamo Canal") and [Salton Sea](/wiki/Salton_Sea "Salton Sea")

The Colorado River delta region in Mexico became a favored place for Americans to invest in agriculture in the late 19th century when Mexican President [Porfirio Díaz](/wiki/Porfirio_D%C3%ADaz "Porfirio Díaz") welcomed foreign capital to develop the country. The Colorado River Land Company, formed by *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* publisher [Harry Chandler](/wiki/Harry_Chandler "Harry Chandler"), his father-in-law [Harrison Gray Otis](/wiki/Harrison_Gray_Otis_(publisher) "Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)"), and others, developed the [Mexicali Valley](/wiki/Mexicali_Valley "Mexicali Valley") in [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") as a thriving land company. The company headquarters was nominally based in Mexico, but its real headquarters was in Los Angeles, California. Land was leased mainly to Americans who were required to develop it. Colorado River water was used to irrigate the rich soil. The company largely escaped the turmoil of the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution") (1910–20), but in the postrevolutionary period, the Mexican government expropriated the company's land to satisfy the demand for [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform_in_Mexico "Land reform in Mexico").[[220]](#cite_note-228)[[221]](#cite_note-229)[[222]](#cite_note-230)

In 1900, the [California Development Company](/wiki/California_Development_Company "California Development Company") (CDC) envisioned irrigating the [Imperial Valley](/wiki/Imperial_Valley "Imperial Valley"), a then dry basin on the California–Mexico border, using water from the Colorado River. Due to the valley's location below sea level, water could be diverted and allowed to flow there entirely by gravity. Engineer [George Chaffey](/wiki/George_Chaffey "George Chaffey") was hired to design the [Alamo Canal](/wiki/Alamo_Canal "Alamo Canal"), which split from the Colorado near [Pilot Knob, California](/wiki/Pilot_Knob_(Imperial_County,_California) "Pilot Knob (Imperial County, California)") and ran south into Mexico, where it joined the [Alamo River](/wiki/Alamo_River "Alamo River"), a dry arroyo which had historically carried overflowing floodwaters from the Colorado into the [Salton Sink](/wiki/Salton_Sink "Salton Sink") at the bottom of Imperial Valley. The scheme worked initially; by 1903, about four thousand people lived in the valley and more than 100,000 acres (40,000 ha) of farmland had been developed.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillingtonJacksonMelosi2005140-231)[[224]](#cite_note-Alamocanal-232)

The Alamo Canal experienced continual problems due to the Colorado's high sediment content and its varying water levels. During low flows, the river often dropped below the level of the canal intake, while high flows silted up the intake, forcing the repeated excavation of new cuts. In early 1905, flooding destroyed the intake gates and water began to flow uncontrolled down the canal towards the Salton Sink. By August, the breach had grown large enough to swallow the entire flow of the river, which began to flood the bottom of the valley. The [Southern Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Southern_Pacific_Railroad "Southern Pacific Railroad") attempted to dam the flow in order to protect their tracks which ran through the valley, but was hampered by repeated flooding.[[224]](#cite_note-Alamocanal-232) It took seven attempts, more than $3 million, and two years for the railroad, the CDC, and the federal government to permanently block the breach and restore the river's original course – but not before part of the Imperial Valley was flooded under a 45-mile-long (72 km) lake, today's [Salton Sea](/wiki/Salton_Sea "Salton Sea"). The Imperial Valley fiasco demonstrated that further economic development of the region would require a dam to control the Colorado's unpredictable flows.[[225]](#cite_note-233)[[226]](#cite_note-234)[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillingtonJacksonMelosi2005141–142-235)

### Boulder Canyon Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Boulder Canyon Project")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/HooverDamConstruction.jpg/250px-HooverDamConstruction.jpg)](/wiki/File:HooverDamConstruction.jpg)

Hoover Dam under construction, 1934

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/IMPERIAL_DAM_TAKES_LAST_OF_COLORADO_RIVER_WATER_FOR_THE_UNITED_STATES._IT_DIVERTS_WATER_INTO_ALL-AMERICAN_CANAL...._-_NARA_-_548847.jpg/250px-IMPERIAL_DAM_TAKES_LAST_OF_COLORADO_RIVER_WATER_FOR_THE_UNITED_STATES._IT_DIVERTS_WATER_INTO_ALL-AMERICAN_CANAL...._-_NARA_-_548847.jpg)](/wiki/File:IMPERIAL_DAM_TAKES_LAST_OF_COLORADO_RIVER_WATER_FOR_THE_UNITED_STATES._IT_DIVERTS_WATER_INTO_ALL-AMERICAN_CANAL...._-_NARA_-_548847.jpg)

The Imperial Dam (bottom right) diverts water into the All-American Canal (center) running towards Imperial Valley.

A large dam on the Colorado River had been envisioned since the 1920s. In 1928, Congress authorized the Reclamation Service (today's [U.S. Bureau of Reclamation](/wiki/U.S._Bureau_of_Reclamation "U.S. Bureau of Reclamation"), or USBR) to build the Boulder Canyon Project, whose key feature would be a dam on the Colorado in Black Canyon 30 miles (48 km) southeast of [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"). On September 30, 1935, [Hoover Dam](/wiki/Hoover_Dam "Hoover Dam") was completed, forming [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead"), capable of holding more than two years of the Colorado's flow. Lake Mead was, and still is, the largest artificial lake in the U.S. by storage capacity.[[25]](#cite_note-LakeMead-25)[[228]](#cite_note-236) The construction of Hoover Dam stabilized the lower channel of the Colorado River, stored water for irrigation in times of drought, captured sediment and controlled floods. Hoover was the tallest dam in the world at the time of construction and also had the world's largest hydroelectric power plant.[[229]](#cite_note-237)

The [Boulder Canyon Project Act](/wiki/Boulder_Canyon_Project_Act "Boulder Canyon Project Act") also authorized the [All-American Canal](/wiki/All-American_Canal "All-American Canal"),[[230]](#cite_note-238) which was built as a permanent replacement for the Alamo Canal and follows a route entirely within the U.S. on its way to the Imperial Valley. The canal's intake is located at [Imperial Dam](/wiki/Imperial_Dam "Imperial Dam"), 20 miles (32 km) above [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"), which diverts the majority of the Colorado's flow with only a small portion continuing to Mexico. With a capacity of over 26,000 cubic feet per second (740 m3/s), the All-American Canal is the largest irrigation canal in the world.[[231]](#cite_note-239) Because the hot, sunny climate lends to a year-round growing season, the Imperial Valley has become one of the most productive farming regions in North America, providing much of the winter produce supply in the U.S.[[6]](#cite_note-ImperialEB-6) The Imperial Irrigation District supplies water to 520,000 acres (210,000 ha) south of the Salton Sea.[[232]](#cite_note-240) The [Coachella Canal](/wiki/Coachella_Canal "Coachella Canal"), which branches northward from the All-American Canal, irrigates another 78,000 acres (32,000 ha) in the [Coachella Valley](/wiki/Coachella_Valley "Coachella Valley").[[233]](#cite_note-241)

[Parker Dam](/wiki/Parker_Dam "Parker Dam") was initially built as the diversion point for the [Colorado River Aqueduct](/wiki/Colorado_River_Aqueduct "Colorado River Aqueduct"), planned by the [Metropolitan Water District of Southern California](/wiki/Metropolitan_Water_District_of_Southern_California "Metropolitan Water District of Southern California") to supply water to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California").[[234]](#cite_note-242) The construction of the dam was opposed by Arizona, which feared that California would take too much water from the Colorado; at one point, Arizona sent members of its [National Guard](/wiki/Arizona_National_Guard "Arizona National Guard") to stop work on the dam. Ultimately, a compromise was reached, with Arizona dropping its objections in exchange for the USBR constructing the Gila Project, which irrigates 110,000 acres (450 km2) on the Arizona side of the river.[[235]](#cite_note-243) By 1941, the 241-mile (388 km) long Colorado River Aqueduct was completed, delivering 1.2 million acre-feet (1.5 km3) of water west to Southern California. The aqueduct enabled the continued growth of Los Angeles and its suburbs, and provides water to about 10 million people today.[[236]](#cite_note-244) The [San Diego Aqueduct](/wiki/San_Diego_Aqueduct "San Diego Aqueduct"), which branches off from the Colorado River Aqueduct in [Riverside County, California](/wiki/Riverside_County,_California "Riverside County, California"), opened in stages between 1954 and 1971 and provides water to another 3 million people in the [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") metro area.[[237]](#cite_note-245)

The Las Vegas Valley of Nevada experienced rapid growth after Hoover Dam, and by 1937 Las Vegas had tapped a pipeline into [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead"). Nevada officials, believing that groundwater resources in the southern part of the state were sufficient for future growth, were more concerned with securing a large amount of the dam's power supply than water from the Colorado; thus they settled for the smallest water allocation of all the states in the Colorado River Compact.[[238]](#cite_note-246) In 2018, due to declining water levels in Lake Mead, a second pipeline was completed with a lower intake elevation.

### Colorado River Storage Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Colorado River Storage Project")]

Main article: [Colorado River Storage Project](/wiki/Colorado_River_Storage_Project "Colorado River Storage Project")

In the first half of the 20th century, the Upper Basin states, with the exception of Colorado, had developed very little of their water allocations from the Colorado River Compact. By the 1950s, however, water demand was rapidly increasing in Utah's [Wasatch Front](/wiki/Wasatch_Front "Wasatch Front") ([Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") metro area) and the [Rio Grande Valley](/wiki/Rio_Grande_Valley_(landform) "Rio Grande Valley (landform)") of New Mexico, which both began exploring ways to divert water from the Colorado Basin.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFradkin1996191-247) The Upper Basin states were concerned that they would not be able to use their full Compact allocations due to increasing water demands in the Lower Basin. The Compact requires the Upper Basin to deliver a minimum annual flow of 7.5 million acre-feet (9.3×109 m3) past Lee's Ferry (measured on a 10-year rolling average). Without additional reservoir storage, the Upper Basin states could not utilize their allocations without impacting water deliveries to the Lower Basin in dry years.[[240]](#cite_note-CRSP-248)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Glen_Canyon_Dam_AZ.jpg/250px-Glen_Canyon_Dam_AZ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glen_Canyon_Dam_AZ.jpg)

Glen Canyon Dam, the largest dam of the Colorado River Storage Project

In 1956 Congress authorized the USBR to construct the Colorado River Storage Project (CRSP), which planned several large reservoirs on the upper Colorado, Green, Gunnison and San Juan Rivers.[[240]](#cite_note-CRSP-248) The initial blueprints for the CRSP included two dams on the Green River within [Echo Park Canyon](/wiki/Echo_Park_(Colorado) "Echo Park (Colorado)") in [Dinosaur National Monument](/wiki/Dinosaur_National_Monument "Dinosaur National Monument") – a move criticized by both the [National Park Service](/wiki/U.S._National_Park_Service "U.S. National Park Service") and environmental groups such as the [Sierra Club](/wiki/Sierra_Club "Sierra Club"). The controversy received nationwide media attention, and the USBR dropped its plans for the Dinosaur dams in exchange for increasing the size of a proposed dam at Glen Canyon.[[241]](#cite_note-249)

The controversy associated with Glen Canyon Dam did not build momentum until construction was well underway. Due to Glen Canyon's remote location, most of the American public did not even know of its existence; the few who did contended that it had much greater scenic value than Echo Park. The [environmental movement](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement") in the American Southwest has opposed the damming and diversion of the Colorado River system due to negative effects on the ecology and natural beauty of the river and its tributaries. During the construction of [Glen Canyon Dam](/wiki/Glen_Canyon_Dam "Glen Canyon Dam") (1956–66), environmental organizations[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] vowed[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] to block any further development of the river, and a number of later dam and aqueduct proposals were defeated by citizen opposition. Sierra Club leader [David Brower](/wiki/David_Brower "David Brower") fought the dam both during the construction and for many years afterwards until his death in 2000. Brower believed that he was personally responsible for the failure to prevent Glen Canyon's flooding, calling it his "greatest mistake, greatest sin".[[242]](#cite_note-250)[[243]](#cite_note-251)

In addition to Glen Canyon Dam, the CRSP includes the [Flaming Gorge Dam](/wiki/Flaming_Gorge_Dam "Flaming Gorge Dam") on the Green River, the [Blue Mesa](/wiki/Blue_Mesa_Dam "Blue Mesa Dam"), [Morrow Point](/wiki/Morrow_Point_Dam "Morrow Point Dam") and [Crystal Dams](/wiki/Crystal_Dam "Crystal Dam") on the Gunnison River, and the [Navajo Dam](/wiki/Navajo_Dam "Navajo Dam") on the San Juan River. A total of 22 "participating projects" (of which 16 have been constructed) were later authorized in order to develop local water supplies at various locations across the Upper Basin states.[[240]](#cite_note-CRSP-248) These include the [Central Utah Project](/wiki/Central_Utah_Project "Central Utah Project"), which delivers 102,000 acre-feet (126,000,000 m3) per year from the Green River basin to the Wasatch Front, and the [San Juan–Chama Project](/wiki/San_Juan%E2%80%93Chama_Project "San Juan–Chama Project"), which diverts 110,000 acre-feet (140,000,000 m3) per year from the San Juan River to the Rio Grande Valley. Both are multi-purpose projects serving a variety of agricultural, municipal and industrial uses.[[244]](#cite_note-252)[[245]](#cite_note-253)

### Pacific Southwest Water Plan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pacific Southwest Water Plan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Arizona_cap_canal.jpg/250px-Arizona_cap_canal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arizona_cap_canal.jpg)

The main canal of the [Central Arizona Project](/wiki/Central_Arizona_Project "Central Arizona Project"), crossing the [Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert")

By the middle of the 20th century, planners were concerned that continued growth in water demand would outstrip the available water supply from the Colorado River. After exploring a multitude of potential projects, the USBR published a study in January 1964 known as the Pacific Southwest Water Plan, which proposed diverting water from the northwestern United States into the Colorado River basin.[[246]](#cite_note-Pacific_Southwest_Water_Plan-254) Arizona's water allocation was a significant focus of the plan, due to the growing concern that its water supply could be curtailed due to California's [seniority of water rights](/wiki/Prior-appropriation_water_rights "Prior-appropriation water rights"). In addition, the plan would guarantee full water supplies to Nevada, California and Mexico, allowing the Upper Basin states to utilize their full allocations without risking reductions in the Lower Basin.[[246]](#cite_note-Pacific_Southwest_Water_Plan-254) The project would cost an estimated $3.1 billion.[[247]](#cite_note-NYT_PSW-255)

The first stage of this plan would divert water from Northern California's [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity_River_(California) "Trinity River (California)"), [Klamath](/wiki/Klamath_River "Klamath River") and [Eel Rivers](/wiki/Eel_River_(California) "Eel River (California)") to Southern California, allowing more Colorado River water to be used, by exchange, in Arizona. A canal system, which ultimately would become the [Central Arizona Project](/wiki/Central_Arizona_Project "Central Arizona Project") (CAP), would be constructed to deliver Arizona's Colorado River allocation to Phoenix and Tucson, both located far away from the Colorado River in the middle of the state. At this point, central Arizona was still entirely dependent on local water supplies, such as the 1911 [Theodore Roosevelt Dam](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Dam "Theodore Roosevelt Dam"),[[248]](#cite_note-256) and was quickly running out of surplus water.[[246]](#cite_note-Pacific_Southwest_Water_Plan-254) Groundwater was pumped at unsustainable rates, and aquifers were collapsing.[[249]](#cite_note-miller-257)

In order to supply the massive amount of power required to pump Colorado River water to central Arizona, two hydroelectric dams were proposed in the Grand Canyon ([Bridge Canyon Dam](/wiki/Bridge_Canyon_Dam "Bridge Canyon Dam") and [Marble Canyon Dam](/wiki/Marble_Canyon_Dam "Marble Canyon Dam")), which while not directly located in [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), would greatly impact flows of the Colorado River through the park.[[246]](#cite_note-Pacific_Southwest_Water_Plan-254) With the controversy over Glen Canyon Dam still ongoing, the public pressure against these dams was immense.[[247]](#cite_note-NYT_PSW-255) As a result, the two Grand Canyon dams were omitted from the final CAP authorization in 1968.[[250]](#cite_note-258) In addition, the boundaries of Grand Canyon National Park were redrawn to prevent future dam projects in the area. The pumping power was replaced by the building of the coal-fired [Navajo Generating Station](/wiki/Navajo_Generating_Station "Navajo Generating Station") near Page, Arizona, in 1976.[[251]](#cite_note-CAP-259)[[252]](#cite_note-260)[[253]](#cite_note-261) In 2019, the Navajo Generating Station ceased operation.[[254]](#cite_note-262)

The CAP was constructed in stages from 1973 to 1993, ultimately extending 336 miles (541 km) from the Colorado River at Parker Dam to [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"). It delivers 1.4 million acre-feet (1.7 km3) of water per year, irrigates 830,000 acres (3,400 km2) of farmland and provides municipal water to about 5 million people.[[251]](#cite_note-CAP-259) Due to financial and environmental concerns,[[249]](#cite_note-miller-257)
most of the facilities proposed in the Pacific Southwest Water Plan were never built (though a smaller version of the Trinity River project was constructed as part of the unrelated [Central Valley Project](/wiki/Central_Valley_Project "Central Valley Project")),[[255]](#cite_note-263) leaving Arizona and Nevada vulnerable to future water reductions under the Compact.[[256]](#cite_note-264)

### Indigenous water rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Indigenous water rights")]

Water rights of Native Americans in the Colorado River basin were largely ignored during the extensive water resources development carried out on the river and its tributaries in the 19th and 20th centuries. The construction of dams has often had negative impacts on tribal peoples, such as the Chemehuevi when their riverside lands were flooded after the completion of [Parker Dam](/wiki/Parker_Dam "Parker Dam") in 1938. Ten Native American tribes in the basin now hold or continue to claim water rights to the Colorado River.[[257]](#cite_note-265) The U.S. government has taken some actions to help quantify and develop the water resources of Native American reservations. The first federally funded irrigation project in the U.S. was the construction of an irrigation canal on the Colorado River Indian Reservation in 1867.[[258]](#cite_note-266) Other water projects include the [Navajo Indian Irrigation Project](/wiki/Navajo_Indian_Irrigation_Project "Navajo Indian Irrigation Project"), authorized in 1962 for the irrigation of lands in part of the Navajo Nation in north-central New Mexico.[[259]](#cite_note-267) The Navajo continue to seek expansion of their water rights because of difficulties with the water supply on their reservation; about 40 percent of its inhabitants must haul water by truck many miles to their homes. In the 21st century, they have filed legal claims against the governments of Arizona, New Mexico and Utah for increased water rights. Some of these claims have been successful for the Navajo, such as a 2004 settlement in which they received a 326,000-acre-foot (402,000 ML) allotment from New Mexico.[[260]](#cite_note-268)

### Post-2000 water supply

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Post-2000 water supply")]

See also: [Southwestern North American megadrought](/wiki/Southwestern_North_American_megadrought "Southwestern North American megadrought")

> [The Colorado is] a 'deficit' river, as if the river were somehow at fault for its overuse.

— [Marc Reisner](/wiki/Marc_Reisner "Marc Reisner"), in *[Cadillac Desert](/wiki/Cadillac_Desert "Cadillac Desert")*[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReisner1993121-269)

When the Colorado River Compact was drafted in the 1920s, it was based on barely 30 years of streamflow records that suggested an average annual flow of 17.5 million acre-feet (21.6 km3) past Lee's Ferry.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReisner1993126-270) Modern studies of [tree rings](/wiki/Tree_ring "Tree ring") revealed that those three decades were probably the wettest in the past 500 to 1,200 years and that the natural long-term annual flow past Lee's Ferry is probably closer to 13.5 million acre-feet (16.7 km3),[[263]](#cite_note-271)[[n 9]](#cite_note-273) with a natural flow at the mouth around 16.3 million acre-feet (20.1 km3).[[265]](#cite_note-Nowak-274) This has resulted in more water being allocated to river users than actually exists in the Colorado.[[266]](#cite_note-Gelt-275) Droughts have exacerbated the issue of water over-allocation.[[267]](#cite_note-276)[[268]](#cite_note-277)

[![View of a reservoir where the water level has dropped, showing white deposits on the surrounding mountains](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Lake_Mead_October_2010.jpg/250px-Lake_Mead_October_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Mead_October_2010.jpg)

Lake Mead in 2010, showing the "bathtub ring" left behind by low water levels

The most severe drought on record, the [southwestern North American megadrought](/wiki/Southwestern_North_American_megadrought "Southwestern North American megadrought"), began in the early 21st century, in which the river basin has produced above-average runoff in only five years between 2000 and 2021.[[269]](#cite_note-278) The region is experiencing a warming trend, which is accompanied by earlier snowmelt, lower precipitation and greater [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration"). A 2004 study showed that a 1–6 percent decrease of precipitation would lead to runoff declining by as much as 18 percent by 2050.[[270]](#cite_note-279)

Since 2000, reservoir levels have fluctuated greatly from year to year, but have experienced a steady long-term decline.[[271]](#cite_note-280) The particularly dry spell between 2000 and 2004 brought Lake Powell to just a third of capacity in 2005, the lowest level on record since initial filling in 1969.[[272]](#cite_note-281) In late 2010, Lake Mead was approaching the "drought trigger" elevation of 1,075 feet (328 m), at which water supplies to Arizona and Nevada would be reduced in accordance with the Colorado River Compact.[[273]](#cite_note-282) Due to Arizona and Nevada's water rights being junior to California's, their allocations can legally be cut to zero before any reductions are made on the California side.[[266]](#cite_note-Gelt-275)[[274]](#cite_note-283)

A wet winter in 2011 temporarily raised lake levels,[[275]](#cite_note-284)[[276]](#cite_note-285) but dry conditions returned in the next two years.[[277]](#cite_note-286) In 2014, the Bureau of Reclamation cut releases from Lake Powell by 10 percent —the first such reduction since the 1960s, when Lake Powell was being filled for the first time.[[278]](#cite_note-287) This resulted in Lake Mead dropping to its lowest recorded level since 1937, when it was first being filled.[[279]](#cite_note-288)

Water year 2018 had a much lower-than-average snowpack.[[280]](#cite_note-289)[[281]](#cite_note-290) In July 2021, after two more extremely dry winters, Lake Powell fell below the previous low set in 2005. In response, the Bureau of Reclamation began releasing water from upstream reservoirs in order to keep Powell above the minimum level for [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") generation.[[282]](#cite_note-291)[[283]](#cite_note-292) Lake Mead fell below the 1,075-foot (328 m) level expected to trigger federally mandated cuts to Arizona and Nevada's water supplies for the first time in history, and is expected to continue declining into 2022.[[284]](#cite_note-293)

On August 16, 2021, the Bureau of Reclamation released the Colorado River Basin August 2021 24-Month Study, and for the first time declared a shortage and that because of "ongoing historic drought and low runoff conditions in the Colorado River Basin, downstream releases from Glen Canyon Dam and Hoover Dam will be reduced in 2022 due to declining reservoir levels."[[285]](#cite_note-Aug16-294)
The Lower Basin reductions will reduce the annual apportionments – Arizona's by 18 percent, Nevada's by 7 percent, and Mexico's by 5 percent.[[286]](#cite_note-295)

On June 14, 2022, Bureau of Reclamation Commissioner Camille Touton told the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural resources that additional cuts of 2–4 million acre-feet were required to stabilize reservoir levels in 2023. Touton warned that if states were unable to negotiate the requisite cuts the Interior department may use its legal authority to cut releases.[[287]](#cite_note-296) When the states were unable to come to an agreement about how to share the proposed cuts, Reclamation began the legal steps to unilaterally reduce releases from Hoover and Glen Canyon Dams in 2023.[[288]](#cite_note-Reuters-297) As of December 2022 the lower basin states of Nevada, Arizona, and California had not agreed on how to reduce water use by the approximately 30% required to keep levels in lakes Mead and Powell from crashing.[[289]](#cite_note-Las_Vegas_Review_Journal-298) The Bureau of Reclamation had projected that water levels at Lake Powell could fall low enough that by July 2023 Glen Canyon Dam would no longer be able to generate any hydropower, a scenario referred to as "dead pool".[[290]](#cite_note-299) Arizona proposed a plan that severely cut allocations to California, and California responded with a plan that severely cut allocations to Arizona, failing to reach consensus. In April 2023, the federal government proposed cutting allocations to Nevada, Arizona, and California evenly which would cut deliveries by as much as one-quarter to each state, rather than according to senior water rights.[[291]](#cite_note-300)

In May 2023, the states finally reached a temporary agreement to prevent a "dead pool", reducing allocations by 3 million acre-feet over three years (until the end of 2026). 700,000 acre-feet were to be negotiated later among California, Arizona, and Nevada.[[292]](#cite_note-deal-301) The cuts were less than the federal government had demanded, and so further cuts will be needed after 2026.[[292]](#cite_note-deal-301) Fewer cuts were needed in the short term because the Colorado River Basin experienced an unusually rainy and snowy weather in early 2023.[[292]](#cite_note-deal-301)[[293]](#cite_note-302)

The agreement also became easier to negotiate because many cuts are being offset by one-time federal funding.[[294]](#cite_note-303) Billions of dollars in funding for programs in the Colorado River Basin to recycle water, increase efficiency, and competitive grants to pay water rights holders not to use water from the river are being provided by the [Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act](/wiki/Infrastructure_Investment_and_Jobs_Act "Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act") and [Inflation Reduction Act](/wiki/Inflation_Reduction_Act "Inflation Reduction Act"), and other programs funded through the [United States Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") and [United States Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior"). These are projected to reduce demand by hundreds of thousands of acre-feet per year.[[295]](#cite_note-304)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ecology")]

### Wildlife and plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Wildlife and plants")]

Further information: [List of flora of the Lower Colorado River Valley](/wiki/List_of_flora_of_the_Lower_Colorado_River_Valley "List of flora of the Lower Colorado River Valley")

[![View of a wide river flowing through a forested area, with jagged mountains in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Topockgorge.jpg/250px-Topockgorge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Topockgorge.jpg)

Heavily forested banks of the Colorado River near [Topock, Arizona](/wiki/Topock,_Arizona "Topock, Arizona")

The Colorado River and its tributaries often nourish extensive corridors of [riparian](/wiki/Riparian_zone "Riparian zone") growth as they traverse the arid desert regions of the watershed. Although riparian zones represent a relatively small proportion of the basin and have been affected by engineering projects and river diversion in many places, they have the greatest biodiversity of any habitat in the basin.[[296]](#cite_note-305) The most prominent riparian zones along the river occur along the lower Colorado below Davis Dam,[[297]](#cite_note-306) especially in the Colorado River Delta, where riparian areas support 358 species of birds despite the reduction in freshwater flow and invasive plants such as [tamarisk](/wiki/Tamarix "Tamarix") (salt cedar).[[298]](#cite_note-307) Reduction of the delta's size has also threatened animals such as jaguars and the [vaquita](/wiki/Vaquita "Vaquita") porpoise, which is endemic to the gulf.[[299]](#cite_note-308) Human development of the Colorado River has also helped to create new riparian zones by smoothing the river's seasonal flow, notably through the Grand Canyon.[[300]](#cite_note-309)

More than 1,600 species of plants grow in the Colorado River watershed, ranging from the [creosote bush](/wiki/Creosote_bush "Creosote bush"), [saguaro cactus](/wiki/Saguaro_cactus "Saguaro cactus"), and [Joshua trees](/wiki/Joshua_tree "Joshua tree") of the Sonoran and Mojave Deserts to the forests of the Rocky Mountains and other uplands, composed mainly of [ponderosa pine](/wiki/Ponderosa_pine "Ponderosa pine"), [subalpine fir](/wiki/Subalpine_fir "Subalpine fir"), [Douglas-fir](/wiki/Douglas-fir "Douglas-fir") and [Engelmann spruce](/wiki/Engelmann_spruce "Engelmann spruce").[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005488-310) Before logging in the 19th century, forests were abundant in high elevations as far south as the Mexico–U.S. border, and runoff from these areas nourished abundant grassland communities in river valleys. Some arid regions of the watershed, such as the upper Green River valley in Wyoming, Canyonlands National Park in Utah and the [San Pedro River](/wiki/San_Pedro_River_(Arizona) "San Pedro River (Arizona)") valley in Arizona and Sonora, supported extensive reaches of grassland roamed by large mammals such as [buffalo](/wiki/American_bison "American bison") and [antelope](/wiki/Pronghorn "Pronghorn") as late as the 1860s. Near Tucson, Arizona, "where now there is only powder-dry desert, the grass once reached as high as the head of a man on horse back".[[302]](#cite_note-311)

Rivers and streams in the Colorado basin were once home to 49 species of native fish, of which 42 were [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_species "Endemic species"). Engineering projects and river regulation have led to the extinction of four species and severe declines in the populations of 40 species.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005493-312) [Bonytail chub](/wiki/Bonytail_chub "Bonytail chub"), [razorback sucker](/wiki/Razorback_sucker "Razorback sucker"), [Colorado pikeminnow](/wiki/Colorado_pikeminnow "Colorado pikeminnow"), and [humpback chub](/wiki/Humpback_chub "Humpback chub") are among those considered the most at risk; all are unique to the Colorado River system and well adapted to the river's natural silty conditions and flow variations. Clear, cold water released by dams has significantly changed characteristics of habitat for these and other Colorado River basin fishes.[[304]](#cite_note-313) A further 40 species that occur in the river today, notably the [brown trout](/wiki/Brown_trout "Brown trout"), were introduced during the 19th and 20th centuries, mainly for sport fishing.[[305]](#cite_note-314) Many fish [native to Arizona](/wiki/List_of_fishes_native_to_Arizona "List of fishes native to Arizona") are threatened or endangered.[[306]](#cite_note-315)

### Impacts of development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Impacts of development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/20230522_Colorado_River_water_usage_-_livestock_crops_etc.svg/250px-20230522_Colorado_River_water_usage_-_livestock_crops_etc.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20230522_Colorado_River_water_usage_-_livestock_crops_etc.svg)

Most of the Colorado River basin water used by humans is used to grow feed for livestock—more than four times the amount used for crops for direct human consumption.[[307]](#cite_note-NYTimes_20230522-316)

[![View of a narrow green river flowing between high, reddish-brown cliffs](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Horseshoebend_smt.jpg/250px-Horseshoebend_smt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horseshoebend_smt.jpg)

The Colorado was named for the reddish color caused by its natural sediment loads, but damming the river has caused it to acquire a clear green hue as seen here in lower Glen Canyon.

Historically, the Colorado [transported](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") from 85 to 100 million short tons (77,000,000 to 91,000,000 t) of sediment or silt to the Gulf of California each year – second only to the Mississippi among North American rivers.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta2007200-317) This sediment nourished wetlands and riparian areas along the river's lower course, particularly in its 3,000-square-mile (7,800 km2) delta, once the largest desert estuary on the continent.[[309]](#cite_note-318) Currently, the majority of sediments carried by the Colorado River are deposited at the upper end of Lake Powell, and most of the remainder ends up in Lake Mead. Various estimates place the time it would take for Powell to completely fill with silt at 300 to 700 years. Dams trapping sediment not only pose damage to river habitat but also threaten future operations of the Colorado River reservoir system.[[310]](#cite_note-319)

Reduction in flow caused by dams, diversions, water for thermoelectric power stations,[[311]](#cite_note-320) and [evaporation](/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation") losses from reservoirs – the latter of which consumes more than 15 percent of the river's natural runoff[[312]](#cite_note-321)—has had severe ecological consequences in the Colorado River Delta and the Gulf of California. Historically, the delta with its large freshwater outflow and extensive salt marshes provided an important breeding ground for aquatic species in the Gulf. Today's desiccated delta, at only a fraction of its former size, no longer provides suitable habitat, and populations of fish, shrimp and sea mammals in the gulf have seen a dramatic decline.[[196]](#cite_note-Controversy-204) Since 1963, the only times when the Colorado River has reached the ocean have been during [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") events in the 1980s and 1990s.[[313]](#cite_note-Landmark-322)

Reduced flows have led to increases in the concentration of certain substances in the lower river that have impacted water quality. Salinity is one of the major issues and also leads to the corrosion of pipelines in agricultural and urban areas.[[314]](#cite_note-:0-323) The lower Colorado's salt content was about 50 parts per million (ppm) in its natural state,[[196]](#cite_note-Controversy-204) but by the 1960s, it had increased to well over 2000 ppm.[[315]](#cite_note-324) By the early 1970s, there was also serious concern about salinity caused by salts leached from local soils by irrigation drainage water, which were estimated to add 10 million short tons (9,100,000 t) of excess salt to the river per year. The [Colorado River Basin Salinity Control Act](/wiki/Colorado_River_Basin_Salinity_Control_Program "Colorado River Basin Salinity Control Program") was passed in 1974, mandating conservation practices including the reduction of saline drainage. The program reduced the annual load by about 1.2 million short tons (1,100,000 t), but salinity remains an ongoing issue.[[316]](#cite_note-CRBSCP-325) In 1997, the USBR estimated that saline irrigation water caused crop damages exceeding $500 million in the U.S. and $100 million in Mexico. Further efforts have been made to combat the salt issue in the lower Colorado, including the construction of a [desalination plant](/wiki/Desalination_plant "Desalination plant") at Yuma.[[317]](#cite_note-326) In 2011, the seven U.S. states agreed upon a "Plan of Implementation", which aims to reduce salinity by 644,000 short tons (584,000 t) per year by 2030.[[316]](#cite_note-CRBSCP-325) In 2013, the Bureau of Reclamation estimated that around $32 million was spent each year to prevent around 1.2 million tons of salt from entering and damaging the Colorado River.[[314]](#cite_note-:0-323)

Agricultural runoff containing pesticide residues has also been concentrated in the lower river in greater amounts. This has led to fish kills; six of these events were recorded between 1964 and 1968 alone.[[318]](#cite_note-327) The pesticide issue is even greater in streams and water bodies near agricultural lands irrigated by the [Imperial Irrigation District](/wiki/Imperial_Irrigation_District "Imperial Irrigation District") with Colorado River water. In the Imperial Valley, Colorado River water used for irrigation overflows into the [New](/wiki/New_River_(Mexico-United_States) "New River (Mexico-United States)") and Alamo rivers and into the Salton Sea. Both rivers and the sea are among the most polluted bodies of water in the United States, posing dangers not only to aquatic life but to contact by humans and migrating birds.[[319]](#cite_note-328)[[320]](#cite_note-329) Pollution from agricultural runoff is not limited to the lower river; the issue is also significant in upstream reaches such as Colorado's Grand Valley, also a major center of irrigated agriculture.[[321]](#cite_note-330)

Large dams such as Hoover and Glen Canyon typically release water from lower levels of their reservoirs, resulting in stable and relatively cold year-round temperatures in long reaches of the river. The Colorado's average temperature once ranged from 85 °F (29 °C) at the height of summer to near freezing in winter, but modern flows through the Grand Canyon, for example, rarely deviate significantly from 46 °F (8 °C).[[322]](#cite_note-331) Changes in temperature regime have caused declines of native fish populations, and stable flows have enabled increased vegetation growth, obstructing riverside habitat.[[323]](#cite_note-332) These flow patterns have also made the Colorado more dangerous to recreational boaters; people are more likely to die of hypothermia in the colder water, and the general lack of flooding allows rockslides to build up, making the river more difficult to navigate.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGhiglieriMyers2001194–214-333)

#### Minute 319

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Minute 319")]

In the 21st century, there has been renewed interest in restoring a limited water flow to the delta. In November 2012, the U.S. and Mexico reached an agreement, known as Minute 319, permitting Mexico storage of its water allotment in U.S. reservoirs during wet years, thus increasing the efficiency with which the water can be used. In addition to renovating irrigation canals in the Mexicali Valley to reduce leakage, this will make about 45,000 acre-feet (56,000,000 m3) per year available for release to the delta on average. The water will be used to provide both an annual base flow and a spring "pulse flow" to mimic the river's original snowmelt-driven regime.[[325]](#cite_note-334)[[326]](#cite_note-335) The first pulse flow, an eight-week release of 105,000 acre-feet (130,000,000 m3), was initiated on March 21, 2014, with the aim of revitalising 2,350 acres (950 hectares) of [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland").[[327]](#cite_note-Pulse-336) This pulse reached the sea on May 16, 2014, marking the first time in 16 years that any water from the Colorado flowed into the ocean, and was hailed as "an experiment of historic political and ecological significance" and a landmark in U.S.–Mexican cooperation in conservation.[[9]](#cite_note-Sacred-9)[[328]](#cite_note-337)[[329]](#cite_note-338) The pulse will be followed by the steady release of 52,000 acre-feet (64,000,000 m3) over the following three years, just a small fraction of its average flow before damming.[[327]](#cite_note-Pulse-336)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colorado_River&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Recreation")]

[![View of two small boats in a river, with high cliffs rising immediately behind them](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Colorado_River_Runners.jpg/250px-Colorado_River_Runners.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colorado_River_Runners.jpg)

A rafting party on the Colorado River

Famed for its dramatic rapids and canyons, the Colorado is one of the most well-known [whitewater](/wiki/Whitewater "Whitewater") rivers in the United States, and its [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") section—run by more than 22,000 people annually[[330]](#cite_note-339)—has been called the "granddaddy of rafting trips".[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlondDunfordSchulte-Peevers200887-340) Grand Canyon trips typically begin at [Lee's Ferry](/wiki/Lee%27s_Ferry "Lee's Ferry") and take out at [Diamond Creek](/wiki/Diamond_Creek_(Arizona) "Diamond Creek (Arizona)") or [Lake Mead](/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead"); they range from one to eighteen days for commercial trips and from two to twenty-five days for private trips.[[332]](#cite_note-341) Private (noncommercial) trips are extremely difficult to arrange because the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") limits river traffic for environmental purposes; people who desire such a trip often have to wait more than 10 years for the opportunity.[[333]](#cite_note-342)

Several other sections of the river and its tributaries are popular whitewater runs, and many of these are also served by commercial outfitters. The Colorado's Cataract Canyon and many reaches in the Colorado headwaters are even more heavily used than the Grand Canyon, and about 60,000 boaters run a single 4.5-mile (7.2 km) section above [Radium, Colorado](/wiki/Radium,_Colorado "Radium, Colorado"), each year.[[334]](#cite_note-uppercorapids-343) The upper Colorado also includes many of the river's most challenging rapids, including those in Gore Canyon, which is considered so dangerous that "boating is not recommended".[[334]](#cite_note-uppercorapids-343) Another section of the river above Moab, known as the Colorado "Daily" or "Fisher Towers Section", is the most visited whitewater run in Utah, with more than 77,000 visitors in 2011 alone.[[335]](#cite_note-344) The rapids of the Green River's Gray and [Desolation](/wiki/Desolation_Canyon "Desolation Canyon") Canyons[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPowell2003345-345) and the less difficult "[Goosenecks](/wiki/Goosenecks_State_Park "Goosenecks State Park")" section of the lower San Juan River are also frequently traversed by boaters.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmstead1997143-346)

Eleven U.S. national parks—[Arches](/wiki/Arches_National_Park "Arches National Park"), [Black Canyon of the Gunnison](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Gunnison_National_Park "Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park"), [Bryce Canyon](/wiki/Bryce_Canyon_National_Park "Bryce Canyon National Park"), [Canyonlands](/wiki/Canyonlands_National_Park "Canyonlands National Park"), [Capitol Reef](/wiki/Capitol_Reef_National_Park "Capitol Reef National Park"), [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), [Mesa Verde](/wiki/Mesa_Verde_National_Park "Mesa Verde National Park"), [Petrified Forest](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park"), [Rocky Mountain](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_National_Park "Rocky Mountain National Park"), [Saguaro](/wiki/Saguaro_National_Park "Saguaro National Park"), and [Zion](/wiki/Zion_National_Park "Zion National Park")—are in the watershed, in addition to many national forests, state parks, and recreation areas.[[338]](#cite_note-347) Hiking, backpacking, camping, skiing, and fishing are among the multiple recreation opportunities offered by these areas. Fisheries have declined in many streams in the watershed, especially in the Rocky Mountains, because of polluted runoff from mining and agricultural activities.[[339]](#cite_note-348) The Colorado's major reservoirs are also heavily traveled summer destinations. Houseboating and water-skiing are popular activities on Lakes Mead, Powell, Havasu, and Mojave, as well as [Flaming Gorge Reservoir](/wiki/Flaming_Gorge_Reservoir "Flaming Gorge Reservoir") in Utah and Wyoming, and [Navajo Reservoir](/wiki/Navajo_Reservoir "Navajo Reservoir") in New Mexico and Colorado. Lake Powell and surrounding [Glen Canyon National Recreation Area](/wiki/Glen_Canyon_National_Recreation_Area "Glen Canyon National Recreation Area") received more than two million visitors per year in 2007,[[340]](#cite_note-349) while nearly 7.9 million people visited Lake Mead and the [Lake Mead National Recreation Area](/wiki/Lake_Mead_National_Recreation_Area "Lake Mead National Recreation Area") in 2008.[[341]](#cite_note-350) Colorado River recreation employs some 250,000 people and contributes $26 billion each year to the Southwest economy.[[342]](#cite_note-351)

The stream gauge here measures flow from an area of 44,850 square miles (116,200 km2), representing about 93.2 percent of the basin.[[43]](#cite_note-Greendischarge-43)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** Before large irrigation and municipal diversions, the Gila River discharged about 1.3 million acre-feet (1.6 km3) per year,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenkeCushing2005514-40) which equals a flow of nearly 2,000 cubic feet per second (57 m3/s).
3. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** Discharge data is for [Bluff, Utah](/wiki/Bluff,_Utah "Bluff, Utah"), located about 113.5 miles (182.7 km) above the confluence with the Colorado. The gauge measures flow from an area of 23,000 square miles (60,000 km2), about 93.5 percent of the basin.[[46]](#cite_note-SJdischarge-48)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-57)** Discharge data is for [Littlefield, Arizona](/wiki/Littlefield,_Arizona "Littlefield, Arizona"), about 66 miles (106 km) from the confluence with the Colorado, and also upstream of the confluence with its major tributary, the [Muddy River](/wiki/Muddy_River_(Nevada) "Muddy River (Nevada)"). The gauge measures flow from an area of 5,090 square miles (13,200 km2), about 39.1 percent of the total basin.[[53]](#cite_note-Virgindischarge-56)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** American population (9.7 million) calculated from statistics from the U.S. Census Bureau[[66]](#cite_note-70) and the State of Colorado.[[67]](#cite_note-SalinityControl-71) The population in Mexico is about 3 million.[[68]](#cite_note-72)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-76)** Unimpaired runoff figures published by the Bureau of Reclamation are an estimate of unregulated river flow without any upstream water consumption.[[70]](#cite_note-USBR_natural_flow_data-75)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-105)** *NIB* = "Northerly International Boundary", or the point at which the Colorado begins to form the Mexico–U.S. border, south of Yuma. 8. **[^](#cite_ref-200)** The average discharge of the Colorado (Grand) River at [Cisco, Utah](/wiki/Cisco,_Utah "Cisco, Utah"), about 97 miles (156 km) upstream from the Green River confluence, is 7,181 cubic feet per second (203.3 m3/s); between here and the confluence, only a few small, intermittent tributaries join the river.[[95]](#cite_note-Ciscodischarge-101)The [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River_tributary) "Green River (Colorado River tributary)") has an average discharge of 6,048 cubic feet per second (171.3 m3/s) as measured at [Green River, Utah](/wiki/Green_River,_Utah "Green River, Utah"), about 117.6 miles (189.3 km) above the confluence;[[43]](#cite_note-Greendischarge-43) below here the only major tributary is the [San Rafael River](/wiki/San_Rafael_River "San Rafael River"), which contributes an average of 131 cubic feet per second (3.7 m3/s), resulting in a total of 6,169 cubic feet per second (174.7 m3/s), still significantly lower than the discharge of the Colorado at their confluence.[[192]](#cite_note-199)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-273)** The discrepancy between the natural flow at Lee's Ferry (13.5 million acre-feet or 16.65 km3) and the gauged flow between 1922 and 2020 (10.58 million acre-feet or 13.05 km3)[[72]](#cite_note-LeesFerryMonthly-78) is mostly due to water diversions above Lee's Ferry and evaporation from reservoirs, especially Lake Powell.[[264]](#cite_note-272)
